questions.learningObjective: Identify the repair station performance regulation
questions.fullObjective: State that 14 CFR Part 145 is the regulation that outlines performance rules for maintenance that a repair station must follow.

flashcard.definition: The FAA regulation that establishes performance rules and certification requirements for repair stations performing maintenance on aircraft and aircraft components.
flashcard.analogy: Like a restaurant health code — it sets the mandatory operating standards a shop must meet to stay certified.

jeopardy.question: This FAA regulation sets the performance rules that a certificated repair station must follow.
choices: 14 CFR Part 91
14 CFR Part 43
14 CFR Part 65
14 CFR Part 145
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 specifically governs certificated repair stations — their certification, personnel, facilities, and operational standards. Part 43 covers how maintenance is performed by anyone authorized; Part 91 covers aircraft operating rules; Part 65 covers airmen certificates. A common mistake is choosing Part 43, but Part 43 governs the work itself, not the repair station organization.

multipleChoice.question: A new hire asks which regulation the shop operates under as an FAA-certificated repair station. What is the correct answer?
choices: 14 CFR Part 145
14 CFR Part 91
14 CFR Part 65
14 CFR Part 43
multipleChoice.explanation: 14 CFR Part 145 is the governing regulation for certificated repair stations. Part 43 sets maintenance performance standards broadly for anyone performing maintenance — it governs the work, not the shop's certification. Part 91 covers aircraft operations. Part 65 covers airmen certification.

practice.question: An FAA inspector asks your shop supervisor which regulation defines the performance rules your repair station must follow. The correct response is:
choices: 14 CFR Part 145 — it specifically governs certificated repair stations
14 CFR Part 91 — it covers all aircraft equipment and maintenance requirements
14 CFR Part 65 — it governs the certificates held by our technicians
14 CFR Part 43 — it governs all maintenance performance standards
practice.explanation: 14 CFR Part 145 governs the repair station as an organization — certification, personnel requirements, facilities, quality control, and operational standards. Part 43 governs how maintenance work must be performed (applicable to all authorized persons), while Part 145 governs the station's certification and operation specifically.

preassessment.question: What regulation governs FAA-certificated repair stations?
choices: 14 CFR Part 43
14 CFR Part 91
14 CFR Part 65
14 CFR Part 145

questions.learningObjective: Explain the purpose of FAA Form 8130-3
questions.fullObjective: Explain the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3 as a document used to certify airworthiness approval and trace the origin of an aircraft part or component.

flashcard.definition: An Airworthiness Approval Tag used to certify that an aircraft part or component meets airworthiness standards and to trace its origin and maintenance history.
flashcard.analogy: Like a birth certificate and health record combined — it proves where the part came from and that it has been properly vetted.

jeopardy.question: This FAA document certifies airworthiness approval and is used to trace the origin of an aircraft part or component.
choices: FAA Form 8110-3
FAA Form 337
FAA Form 8130-3
FAA Form 8060-4
jeopardy.explanation: Form 8130-3 is the Airworthiness Approval Tag. Form 337 documents major repairs and alterations. Form 8110-3 is a Statement of Compliance for TSO articles. Form 8060-4 is an Airman Certificate.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3?
choices: To document a major repair or alteration on an aircraft
To certify airworthiness approval and trace the origin of a part or component
To authorize a repair station to perform a specific type of maintenance
To record the results of a transponder inspection
multipleChoice.explanation: Form 8130-3 (Airworthiness Approval Tag) certifies that a part meets airworthiness standards and traces its origin and history. Form 337 documents major repairs and alterations. Operations specifications authorize station work. Inspection results go in the maintenance record.

practice.question: A part arrives with an FAA Form 8130-3 attached. A new tech asks what this document tells them. The correct answer is:
choices: It shows the part was manufactured before a specific cutoff date
It confirms the part is approved as airworthy and its origin is traceable
It proves the part has passed a 24-month inspection cycle
It authorizes the part for military aircraft use only
practice.explanation: Form 8130-3 is the Airworthiness Approval Tag. Its presence confirms the part has been certified as meeting airworthiness standards and that its origin is documented and traceable — which is critical for parts installed on certificated aircraft.

preassessment.question: FAA Form 8130-3 is used to:
choices: Certify airworthiness and trace a part's origin
Record a major alteration
Authorize a return-to-service
Document a pre-employment drug test

questions.learningObjective: State required elements of a maintenance record per §43.9
questions.fullObjective: State the definition of a maintenance record per 14 CFR §43.9, including the required elements: description of work, date, name, certificate number, and return-to-service signature.

flashcard.definition: Per 14 CFR §43.9, a maintenance record must include: description of work performed, date completed, name of person performing work, certificate number, and a return-to-service signature.
flashcard.analogy: Like a doctor's note after surgery — it must say what was done, when, by whom with their license number, and that the patient is cleared to go.

jeopardy.question: Per 14 CFR §43.9, this element is NOT required in a maintenance record entry.
choices: Description of work performed
Aircraft registration number
Technician's certificate number
Return-to-service signature
jeopardy.explanation: §43.9 requires: description of work, date, name, certificate number, and return-to-service signature. The aircraft registration number is not a §43.9 required element — the five required elements describe who did the work and what was done, not the aircraft identifier.

multipleChoice.question: Per 14 CFR §43.9, which elements must be included in a maintenance record entry?
choices: Description of work, date, name, certificate number, and return-to-service signature
Description of work, aircraft serial number, date, and inspector signature
Work order number, date, technician photo ID, and certificate number
Part numbers replaced, date, flight hours, and technician name
multipleChoice.explanation: §43.9 requires five elements: description of work performed, date completed, name, certificate number, and return-to-service signature. Aircraft serial number, flight hours, and photo ID are not required elements.

practice.question: A tech completes an avionics installation and writes: 'Installed GPS unit per STC. Aircraft N12345. Signed, J. Smith.' What critical §43.9 elements are missing?
choices: The work order number and STC approval reference number
The aircraft manufacturer model designation
The aircraft's total flight hours at time of work
The date, certificate number, and return-to-service statement
practice.explanation: The entry is missing three of five required §43.9 elements: the date, the technician's certificate number, and the return-to-service statement. The aircraft N-number and STC reference are not §43.9 requirements.

preassessment.question: Which is a required element of a maintenance record per 14 CFR §43.9?
choices: Aircraft's flight hours
Aircraft registration number
Technician's certificate number
Work order reference number

questions.learningObjective: State which regulations authorize repair stations for pitot-static and transponder inspections
questions.fullObjective: State that appropriately rated repair stations are authorized to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections under 14 CFR 91.411 and 91.413.

flashcard.definition: Appropriately rated repair stations are authorized to perform pitot-static system inspections (91.411) and transponder inspections (91.413).
flashcard.analogy: Like a specialty clinic being authorized for specific procedures — the facility's rating matters, not just the individual practitioner's credentials.

jeopardy.question: These two regulations authorize appropriately rated repair stations to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections.
choices: 14 CFR Part 43 and Part 145
jeopardy.explanation: 91.411 covers altimeter/pitot-static system testing and 91.413 covers transponder testing. Both authorize appropriately rated repair stations. 91.207 covers ELTs. 91.171/177 cover VOR checks and minimum altitudes.

multipleChoice.question: Under which regulations are appropriately rated repair stations authorized to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections?
choices: 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A and B
multipleChoice.explanation: 91.411 requires altimeter and pitot-static system tests every 24 months. 91.413 requires transponder tests every 24 months. Both may be performed by appropriately rated repair stations. 91.207 covers ELTs. 91.171 covers VOR checks.

practice.question: A customer asks if your repair station is authorized to perform the required transponder and pitot-static checks. The correct response is:
choices: Only an FAA Flight Standards office can specifically authorize these inspections for each job
Yes — 14 CFR 91.411 and 91.413 authorize appropriately rated repair stations to perform both
No — only individual A&P mechanics with an IA may perform these inspections, not repair stations
Yes — any avionics shop may perform these checks without holding specific ratings
practice.explanation: 91.411 (pitot-static) and 91.413 (transponder) authorize appropriately rated repair stations to perform these inspections. The 'appropriately rated' qualifier is key — not all shops are automatically authorized. Individual mechanics may also perform these checks.

preassessment.question: Which regulation requires periodic transponder testing that repair stations are authorized to perform?
choices: 14 CFR Part 43

questions.learningObjective: State the pre-employment drug test requirement for safety-sensitive functions
questions.fullObjective: State the requirement that a technician must pass a pre-employment drug test before performing safety-sensitive functions if the repair station's FAA-approved drug program requires it.

flashcard.definition: A technician must pass a pre-employment drug test before performing safety-sensitive functions if the repair station's FAA-approved drug testing program requires it.
flashcard.analogy: Like a medical clearance before starting a healthcare job — you must be cleared before any safety-sensitive work begins.

jeopardy.question: Before performing safety-sensitive functions at a repair station with an FAA-approved drug program, a new hire must pass this.
choices: A background check
A pre-employment drug test
An FAA practical exam
A 30-day probationary review
jeopardy.explanation: FAA regulations require passing a pre-employment drug test before performing safety-sensitive maintenance functions when the station has an FAA-approved drug testing program. Background checks, practical exams, and probationary reviews are not the regulated requirement.

multipleChoice.question: When must a technician pass a pre-employment drug test before performing safety-sensitive functions at a repair station?
choices: Only if the technician has a prior drug offense on record
Only after completing 90 days of employment and a probationary period
If the repair station's FAA-approved drug program requires it
Only when assigned to work on commercial transport category aircraft
multipleChoice.explanation: The requirement applies when the repair station operates an FAA-approved drug testing program. Prior offenses, probation periods, and aircraft type do not determine when testing is required.

practice.question: A new tech has been hired at a Part 145 shop with an FAA-approved drug program. They hold a valid A&P certificate. When can they begin performing safety-sensitive maintenance?
choices: Immediately — their A&P certificate verifies their professional qualifications
After a 30-day orientation period regardless of any testing requirement
Only after passing the pre-employment drug test required by the station's program
After the IA supervises their first five jobs to confirm technical competency
practice.explanation: When a station has an FAA-approved drug program, passing the pre-employment drug test is required before performing safety-sensitive functions. An A&P certificate demonstrates qualifications but does not waive the drug testing requirement.

preassessment.question: At a repair station with an FAA-approved drug program, a new hire must pass _____ before performing safety-sensitive work.
choices: A practical skills exam
A pre-employment drug test
An FAA medical examination
A 60-day background check

questions.learningObjective: Document altimeter and transponder test results per Part 43
questions.fullObjective: Document altimeter and transponder test results correctly in maintenance records, including test date, equipment used, results, and return-to-service statement per 14 CFR Part 43.

flashcard.definition: Per 14 CFR Part 43, test records must include: test date, equipment used (with calibration status), test results, and a return-to-service statement.
flashcard.analogy: Like a lab report — you record not just the result but the tools used and the date, so the work can be verified or reproduced later.

jeopardy.question: Per 14 CFR Part 43, this information is required in a transponder test record but is most often omitted by technicians.
choices: The specific test equipment used
The aircraft owner's signature
The aircraft's total flight hours
The repair station's work order number
jeopardy.explanation: Part 43 requires the specific test equipment used to be documented — allowing verification that calibrated, appropriate equipment was actually used. Aircraft owner signature, flight hours, and work order numbers are not required transponder test record elements.

multipleChoice.question: What must be included in a maintenance record for an altimeter or transponder test per 14 CFR Part 43?
choices: Test date, equipment used, results, and return-to-service statement
Test date, aircraft serial number, pilot signature, and results
Equipment model number, calibration sticker photo, and next due date
Work order number, technician photo ID, and pass/fail result only
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 43 requires four elements for test records: test date, equipment used (including calibration status), test results, and a return-to-service statement. Aircraft serial number, pilot signature, and work order number are not mandatory elements.

practice.question: You've completed a 91.413 transponder check. Which log entry is fully compliant with Part 43 documentation requirements?
choices: Transponder check — passed. Signed off. N12345.
Transponder test per 91.413. Date: 2026-02-23. IFR-6000 (cal. due 2026-08-01). Results within limits. Aircraft approved RTS. J. Doe #IA-54321.
Xpdr test complete. Equipment calibrated. Good to go.
Transponder inspected and found operational. Return to service.
practice.explanation: Only option B contains all required elements: regulatory reference, date, specific equipment with calibration status, test results, return-to-service statement, and technician name with certificate number. All other options omit critical elements.

preassessment.question: Per Part 43, which element must appear in a transponder test record?
choices: Aircraft owner's signature
Next scheduled test date
Test equipment used
Work order number

questions.learningObjective: State the typical use of mono audio in GA aircraft
questions.fullObjective: State the typical use of mono audio in general aviation aircraft audio systems: single-channel intercom and speaker monitoring applications.

flashcard.definition: Mono (single-channel) audio is typically used in general aviation aircraft for intercom communications and speaker monitoring — a single audio channel routed to all headsets and speakers.
flashcard.analogy: Like an old AM radio — one audio channel broadcast to all listeners, versus stereo which has distinct left and right channels.

jeopardy.question: In general aviation aircraft, mono audio is primarily used for these two applications.
choices: Engine monitoring and weather display
Single-channel intercom and speaker monitoring
ATC recording and cabin entertainment
Flight director cues and GPWS alerts
jeopardy.explanation: GA audio systems use mono (single-channel) audio for intercom communications and speaker monitoring. Stereo separation provides no operational benefit for ATC communications or crew intercom — the same audio signal goes to all headsets and speakers.

multipleChoice.question: What is the typical use of mono audio in general aviation aircraft audio systems?
choices: Stereo entertainment for cabin passengers
Dedicated frequency isolation for pilot and co-pilot separately
Digital audio databus communications between avionics units
Single-channel intercom and speaker monitoring
multipleChoice.explanation: GA audio systems use mono (single-channel) audio for intercom and speaker monitoring. Stereo is unnecessary for ATC communications or crew intercom — the same audio signal goes to all headsets and speakers. Stereo entertainment and digital databuses are different systems.

practice.question: A student asks why a GA aircraft audio panel uses mono rather than stereo audio. The most accurate explanation is:
choices: FAA regulations prohibit stereo audio in certified cockpits
Stereo equipment exceeds weight limitations for small aircraft certification
Single-channel audio is sufficient for intercom and speaker monitoring in GA operations
Mono is mandated by all applicable TSOs for avionics audio outputs
practice.explanation: GA intercom and speaker monitoring are single-channel applications — all crew hear the same audio signal. Stereo separation provides no operational benefit for ATC communications or crew intercom. There is no FAA prohibition on stereo, and weight is not the primary regulatory consideration.

preassessment.question: Mono audio in a GA aircraft is primarily used for:
choices: Entertainment streaming
Weather radar alerts
Intercom and speaker monitoring
Digital databus output

questions.learningObjective: Explain why clean work areas are critical in a Part 145 shop
questions.fullObjective: Explain why keeping walkways and work areas free of clutter in a Part 145 repair station is critical for preventing FOD, trip hazards, and tool loss.

flashcard.definition: Keeping walkways and work areas clutter-free in a Part 145 shop prevents FOD (Foreign Object Damage/Debris), trip hazards for personnel, and tool loss — all of which directly affect airworthiness and safety.
flashcard.analogy: Like a hospital operating room floor — a dropped scalpel (or screw) creates both a personnel hazard and a risk of ending up somewhere it shouldn't.

jeopardy.question: In a Part 145 shop, cluttered walkways are dangerous primarily because of these three hazards.
choices: Fire, flooding, and electrical shock
Fuel contamination, EMI, and oil spills
Noise, vibration, and chemical exposure
FOD, trip hazards, and tool loss
jeopardy.explanation: Clutter creates FOD risk (debris entering aircraft), trip hazards for personnel, and tool accountability failures. These are the core reasons for mandatory housekeeping standards in Part 145 facilities.

multipleChoice.question: Why is keeping walkways and work areas free of clutter critical in a Part 145 repair station?
choices: OSHA requires it primarily for general workplace aesthetics
It prevents FOD, trip hazards, and tool loss that could affect airworthiness and personnel safety
Clutter causes EMI interference with avionics test equipment calibration
It is only required during FAA surveillance visits and scheduled audits
multipleChoice.explanation: Clutter in an avionics shop creates three key risks: FOD (hardware, wire scraps, or tools entering aircraft systems), trip and fall hazards, and tool accountability failures. All three directly impact safety and airworthiness.

practice.question: During a bench job, wire scraps and connector pins accumulate on the floor near the work area. The primary reason this must be cleaned up immediately is:
choices: Wire scraps and pins are classic FOD sources and create trip hazards that can affect airworthiness and personnel safety
The scraps create an electrical hazard if energized test equipment is present in the area
OSHA requires all wire scraps to be placed in a designated bin within 5 minutes of generation
It appears unprofessional when customers are observing the shop floor
practice.explanation: Wire scraps and connector pins are classic FOD sources — if they find their way into an aircraft, they can cause serious airworthiness issues. They also create trip hazards. Cleanliness in a maintenance shop is an airworthiness issue, not just aesthetics.

preassessment.question: Cluttered walkways in a Part 145 shop primarily create a risk of:
choices: EMI interference with test equipment
FOD, trip hazards, and tool loss
Customer complaints during audits
Static discharge to sensitive avionics

questions.learningObjective: State the definition of a major alteration per Part 43 Appendix A
questions.fullObjective: State the definition of a major alteration per 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A as a change to type design that might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or other airworthiness qualities.

flashcard.definition: A change to type design that might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or other airworthiness qualities — and is not listed in the aircraft specifications or type certificate data sheet.
flashcard.analogy: Like a structural renovation changing a building's load-bearing capacity — it needs engineering review and a permit, unlike replacing a light fixture.

jeopardy.question: Per 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A, this type of change to type design might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or airworthiness.
choices: Preventive maintenance
Major alteration
Minor repair
Line replacement
jeopardy.explanation: A major alteration is a type design change that might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or other airworthiness qualities — requiring approved data such as an STC or Form 337.

multipleChoice.question: Per 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A, how is a major alteration defined?
choices: Any repair costing more than $1,000 in parts and labor
Any alteration not specifically covered by the aircraft's POH
A change to type design that might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or other airworthiness qualities
Any repair requiring an IA to approve the return-to-service
multipleChoice.explanation: The regulatory definition focuses on the effect on airworthiness: a type design change that might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or other airworthiness qualities. Cost, POH coverage, and IA involvement are not the determining factors.

practice.question: A shop installs a new avionics system via STC that includes a flight manual supplement modifying the aircraft's operating limitations. This should be classified as:
choices: A minor alteration — avionics are not structural components
A major alteration — the flight manual supplement signals a change affecting airworthiness qualities
Preventive maintenance — STCs can be performed by certificated pilots on their own aircraft
A line replacement — the new unit replaces equipment of the same general type
practice.explanation: When a modification changes operating limitations via a flight manual supplement, it meets the Part 43 Appendix A definition of a major alteration — it affects airworthiness qualities. The fact that it is avionics rather than structural does not make it minor when airworthiness qualities are affected.

preassessment.question: A change to type design that might appreciably affect structural strength or flight characteristics is classified as a:
choices: Major alteration
Major repair
Minor repair
Preventive maintenance item

questions.learningObjective: Determine when maintenance cannot proceed due to missing current technical data
questions.fullObjective: Determine that maintenance cannot be legally performed on a radio listed on the capability list if the current revision of the manufacturer's maintenance manual is not available in the shop.

flashcard.definition: Being on the capability list authorizes the work type, but Part 145 also requires the current revision of the manufacturer's maintenance manual before maintenance begins. Both authorization and current data are required.
flashcard.analogy: Like a surgeon authorized for a procedure — they still must have the current protocol on hand. Credentials alone do not substitute for current guidance.

jeopardy.question: Even if a radio is on the repair station's capability list, maintenance cannot legally proceed without this being available in the shop.
choices: A signed customer work order
The current revision of the manufacturer's maintenance manual
An FAA field approval letter
The aircraft's airworthiness certificate
jeopardy.explanation: Capability list listing provides authorization, but Part 145 also requires current technical data. An outdated manual does not satisfy this requirement — authorization and current data are both required, not interchangeable.

multipleChoice.question: A radio is on your shop's capability list, but only an outdated manual is available. What must happen before maintenance begins?
choices: Proceed — capability list listing is sufficient authorization for the work
Contact the FAA for a field approval to use the outdated manual revision
Obtain the current revision of the manufacturer's maintenance manual first
Have the IA sign off in advance to authorize proceeding without current data
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 145 requires both authorization (capability list) and current technical data. An IA authorization does not substitute for required technical data. The FAA does not issue field approvals to use outdated manufacturer manuals.

practice.question: A tech is assigned to troubleshoot a comm radio on the shop's capability list. The available manual is two revisions old. The correct action is:
choices: Begin work and note the manual revision used in the maintenance record for transparency
Get the current revision before starting — outdated technical data does not meet Part 145 requirements
Ask the IA to authorize proceeding with the available outdated revision
Proceed from experience since the technician is familiar with this radio model
practice.explanation: Part 145 requires current technical data. Outdated revisions may contain critical differences in procedures or safety requirements. There is no regulatory tolerance for outdated data — the technician must obtain the current manual before beginning work.

preassessment.question: If the current revision of the manufacturer's maintenance manual is unavailable, maintenance on a listed radio:
choices: May proceed with IA authorization
May proceed if the technician has prior experience on the model
Can use the previous revision with QC department approval
Cannot legally be performed

questions.learningObjective: State the FAA definition of maintenance
questions.fullObjective: State the FAA regulatory definition of 'maintenance' as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and replacement of parts, but excluding preventive maintenance.

flashcard.definition: Per FAA regulations: inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and replacement of parts — explicitly excluding preventive maintenance, which is a separate regulatory category.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car service package covering inspections and repairs — but not the DIY tasks the owner is allowed to perform themselves.

jeopardy.question: The FAA definition of 'maintenance' includes inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement — but explicitly excludes this.
choices: Alterations
Preventive maintenance
Overhaul
Major repairs
jeopardy.explanation: The FAA defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement. Preventive maintenance is a separate regulatory category limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate — explicitly excluded from the definition of maintenance.

multipleChoice.question: According to FAA regulations, which activity is explicitly EXCLUDED from the definition of 'maintenance'?
choices: Inspection
Preservation
Preventive maintenance
Parts replacement
multipleChoice.explanation: The FAA definition of maintenance lists inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and replacement of parts. Preventive maintenance is carved out as a separate category that certificated pilots may perform on aircraft they own or operate.

practice.question: A certificated private pilot asks if topping off the hydraulic fluid on their own aircraft counts as 'maintenance' under FAA regulations. The correct answer is:
choices: Yes — fluid servicing is preservation, which is included in the FAA maintenance definition
No — it is preventive maintenance, which is explicitly excluded from the FAA definition of maintenance
Yes — any work performed on an aircraft system qualifies as maintenance
No — hydraulic fluid servicing always requires an A&P certificate regardless of ownership
practice.explanation: Preventive maintenance is explicitly excluded from the FAA definition of maintenance. Simple tasks like fluid servicing fall under preventive maintenance — a separate regulatory category that certificated pilots may perform on aircraft they own or operate.

preassessment.question: The FAA definition of maintenance explicitly excludes:
choices: Preventive maintenance
Overhaul
Parts replacement
Inspection

questions.learningObjective: Apply the correct first step when troubleshooting in a Part 145 shop
questions.fullObjective: Apply the correct first step when troubleshooting an intermittent NAV flag on a VOR receiver in a Part 145 shop: consult the manufacturer's maintenance manual before replacing any components.

flashcard.definition: In a Part 145 shop, the first step when troubleshooting any fault is to consult the manufacturer's maintenance manual — approved data must guide the diagnostic procedure before any components are replaced.
flashcard.analogy: Like calling tech support before replacing your router — diagnosis first, hardware swap last.

jeopardy.question: When troubleshooting an intermittent NAV flag on a VOR receiver in a Part 145 shop, this is the required first action before replacing any components.
choices: Swap the receiver with a known-good unit to isolate the LRU
Pull the circuit breaker and reset the system
Consult the manufacturer's maintenance manual
Check the antenna coax cable for continuity
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 requires maintenance to be performed per approved technical data. Consulting the maintenance manual is the mandatory first step — it defines the approved diagnostic procedure. Swapping components without approved data is shotgun troubleshooting and a regulatory violation.

multipleChoice.question: A VOR receiver shows an intermittent NAV flag. What is the correct first step in a Part 145 shop?
choices: Consult the manufacturer's maintenance manual before replacing any components
Swap the receiver with a known-good spare to quickly isolate the fault to the LRU
Check the antenna coax for continuity — it is the most common cause of intermittent flags
Document the fault and return the aircraft to service pending parts availability
multipleChoice.explanation: Approved technical data must guide all maintenance in a Part 145 shop. The manual defines the diagnostic procedure — it may direct checking the antenna first or running a self-test. Jumping to a swap without the manual is a regulatory violation regardless of technician experience.

practice.question: An experienced tech says 'I've seen this VOR flag before — just swap the nav receiver.' You're in a Part 145 shop. What should you do?
choices: Follow the experienced tech's guidance — practical experience outweighs the manual for intermittents
Consult the manufacturer's maintenance manual first to follow the approved diagnostic procedure
Replace the antenna coax first since that is statistically the most common cause
Write up the discrepancy and defer it to the IA without any further investigation
practice.explanation: In a Part 145 shop, maintenance must follow approved technical data. An experienced tech's shortcut may be correct or incorrect — but it is not the approved procedure. The manual defines the correct diagnostic sequence and protects both the aircraft and the technician legally.

preassessment.question: Before replacing any components while troubleshooting in a Part 145 shop, you must first:
choices: Get IA authorization for the component replacement
Verify the aircraft logbook for prior entries on the fault
Check all circuit breakers in the affected system
Consult the manufacturer's maintenance manual

questions.learningObjective: Classify STC installations with flight manual supplements as major alterations
questions.fullObjective: Classify an STC installation that includes a flight manual supplement as a major alteration, regardless of whether the part itself holds a TSO.

flashcard.term: STC + Flight Manual Supplement = Major Alteration
flashcard.definition: An STC installation that includes a flight manual supplement is a major alteration — regardless of the part's TSO status — because it changes the aircraft's approved operating limitations or procedures.
flashcard.analogy: A TSO on the part is like a UL listing on an appliance — the part is approved, but modifying your home's electrical panel to install it still requires a permit.

jeopardy.question: An STC installation includes this document, which classifies the installation as a major alteration regardless of the part's TSO status.
choices: A work order
A flight manual supplement
An AD compliance record
A manufacturer's service letter
jeopardy.explanation: A flight manual supplement changes the aircraft's approved operating limitations or procedures — that change to type design qualifies the installation as a major alteration under Part 43 Appendix A, regardless of the part's TSO approval.

multipleChoice.question: A shop installs an avionics system under an STC. The STC data package includes a flight manual supplement changing VFR-only to IFR-approved operations. This installation should be classified as:
choices: A minor alteration — avionics do not affect structural strength
A major alteration — the flight manual supplement signals a change affecting airworthiness qualities
A line replacement — the part has a TSO so no alteration approval is needed
Preventive maintenance — pilots can perform STC installations on their own aircraft
multipleChoice.explanation: The flight manual supplement signals the change affects aircraft airworthiness qualities (operating limitations). That meets the Part 43 Appendix A major alteration definition. TSO approval on the part does not determine the classification of the installation.

practice.question: A customer's avionics unit has an STC and a current TSO. The STC kit includes a flight manual supplement. A tech says 'the TSO means we log it as a line replacement.' Is the tech correct?
choices: No — the flight manual supplement classifies this as a major alteration regardless of the TSO
Yes — a TSO-approved part installed per STC is always classified as a line replacement
Yes — flight manual supplements only matter for structural modifications, not avionics
No — only an IA can classify STC installations, not the installing technician
practice.explanation: The tech is incorrect. The TSO approves the part's design — it does not determine the classification of the installation. The flight manual supplement is the key indicator: it signals a change affecting operating limitations, making this a major alteration per Part 43 Appendix A.

preassessment.question: An STC installation that includes a flight manual supplement is classified as:
choices: Preventive maintenance
A minor alteration
A major alteration
A line replacement

questions.learningObjective: State that 14 CFR Part 43 governs maintenance performance rules
questions.fullObjective: State that 14 CFR Part 43 is the regulation governing the performance rules for aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations.

flashcard.definition: The FAA regulation governing performance rules for aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, rebuilding, and alterations — applying to any authorized person performing this work, not just repair stations.
flashcard.analogy: If Part 145 is the rulebook for the repair station as an organization, Part 43 is the rulebook for the work itself — it applies to everyone touching the aircraft.

jeopardy.question: This regulation governs performance rules for aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations — applying to any authorized person, not just repair stations.
choices: 14 CFR Part 43
14 CFR Part 65
14 CFR Part 91
14 CFR Part 145
jeopardy.explanation: Part 43 sets the standards for how maintenance work must be performed and applies broadly — to individual mechanics, repair stations, and other authorized persons. Part 145 governs the repair station organization. Part 43 governs the work itself.

multipleChoice.question: Which regulation establishes the performance rules for aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations?
choices: 14 CFR Part 91
14 CFR Part 145
14 CFR Part 43
14 CFR Part 65
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 43 covers how maintenance must be performed — standards, record-keeping, return-to-service requirements — for all authorized persons. Part 145 covers repair station certification. Part 91 covers operations. Part 65 covers airmen certificates.

practice.question: A mechanic working independently (not at a repair station) performs an avionics repair. Which regulation primarily governs the standards for how that work must be performed?
choices: 14 CFR Part 145 — all avionics maintenance must be performed at a certificated repair station
14 CFR Part 65 — it governs the mechanic's certificate and authorized scope
14 CFR Part 43 — it governs maintenance performance standards regardless of who performs the work
14 CFR Part 91 — it covers all aircraft operations and equipment maintenance
practice.explanation: Part 43 applies to all authorized persons performing maintenance — not just repair stations. Part 145 applies to shop certification. Part 65 covers who may hold a certificate. Part 91 covers operations. The how of maintenance performance is always Part 43.

preassessment.question: Performance rules for aircraft maintenance and alterations are found in:
choices: 14 CFR Part 91
14 CFR Part 65
14 CFR Part 145
14 CFR Part 43

questions.learningObjective: Apply correct insertion/extraction tool practice for circular connectors
questions.fullObjective: Apply the correct practice for inserting or extracting terminated wires from standard circular connectors in a Part 145 shop, using the proper extraction and insertion tools to avoid pin damage.

flashcard.definition: Terminated wires must be inserted into or removed from circular connectors using the proper manufacturer-specified insertion and extraction tools — never improvised tools — to prevent pin damage and intermittent faults.
flashcard.analogy: Like removing a SIM card with the proper eject tool — the right tool protects the socket from damage that would be difficult to see and even harder to troubleshoot.

jeopardy.question: When inserting or extracting terminated wires from standard circular connectors, using these prevents pin damage and difficult-to-diagnose intermittent faults.
choices: Needle-nose pliers with padded jaws
Proper manufacturer-specified insertion and extraction tools
A heat gun and connector pick
Connector backshell removal tools
jeopardy.explanation: Manufacturer-specified insertion and extraction tools are required. Improvised tools damage pins, bend contacts, or crack the connector insert — creating intermittent faults that are extremely difficult to diagnose later.

multipleChoice.question: What is the correct practice for inserting or extracting terminated wires from standard circular connectors in a Part 145 shop?
choices: Pull firmly by the wire insulation — circular connectors are designed for hand extraction
Heat the connector body slightly to loosen pin retention before extraction
Use needle-nose pliers with padded jaws to protect the pin during removal
Use the proper insertion and extraction tools specified for that connector series
multipleChoice.explanation: Each connector series has specific insertion and extraction tools. Using the correct tools prevents pin damage, bent contacts, and insert damage. All other options risk damage — pliers bend contacts, heating damages insulation, and pulling by insulation strains the termination.

practice.question: A connector pin needs to be extracted and re-terminated. The proper extraction tool is not immediately available, but needle-nose pliers are nearby. The correct action is:
choices: Use the pliers carefully — experienced technicians do this routinely without causing damage
Push the pin out from the rear of the connector using a pick tool
Cut the wire behind the pin and re-terminate in a new unused connector cavity
Locate the proper extraction tool before proceeding — improvised tools are not acceptable
practice.explanation: Improvising with pliers or picks risks damaged pins, bent contacts, and insert damage that may not be immediately visible. The resulting intermittent fault could be nearly impossible to find later. The correct action is to find the proper tool — non-negotiable per AC 43.13-1B and manufacturer data.

preassessment.question: To extract a terminated wire from a circular connector without causing damage, you must use:
choices: The proper insertion/extraction tool
Needle-nose pliers
A pick and dental tool
Heat and a connector pick

questions.learningObjective: Determine that TSO-for-TSO replacement per approved data is not a major alteration
questions.fullObjective: Determine that replacing a TSO part with another TSO part of the same type is generally not considered a major alteration, provided the replacement is done per approved data.

flashcard.definition: Replacing a TSO part with another TSO part of the same type, done per approved data, is generally NOT a major alteration — it does not change the type design in a way that appreciably affects airworthiness.
flashcard.analogy: Like replacing a UL-listed circuit breaker with another of the same rating per the panel specs — same approval class, done per the approved design data.

jeopardy.question: Replacing a TSO part with another TSO part of the same type, done per approved data, is generally not classified as this.
choices: A major alteration
A line replacement
A major repair
Preventive maintenance
jeopardy.explanation: TSO-for-TSO replacement of the same type per approved data is not a major alteration because it does not appreciably change the aircraft's airworthiness qualities. The two key qualifiers are 'same type' and 'per approved data.'

multipleChoice.question: A technician replaces a TSO-approved comm radio with a different brand but same TSO class unit, per the aircraft manufacturer's approved data. This is classified as:
choices: A major alteration — any avionics change requires FAA Form 337
A major repair — comm systems are flight-critical installed equipment
Generally not a major alteration — it is a TSO-for-TSO replacement per approved data
Preventive maintenance — TSO equipment replacement is owner-authorized
multipleChoice.explanation: TSO-for-TSO replacement per approved data is generally not a major alteration because it does not appreciably change the aircraft's airworthiness qualities. Both qualifiers matter: same TSO class and done per approved data.

practice.question: A customer wants to upgrade their VHF comm from an older TSO unit to a newer TSO unit of the same class, per the aircraft manufacturer's installation manual. Does this require a Form 337?
choices: Yes — any radio replacement is a major alteration requiring Form 337
Yes — different TSO revision levels always trigger major alteration classification
No — only structural changes ever require a Form 337 to be filed
No — TSO-for-TSO replacement per approved data is generally not a major alteration
practice.explanation: TSO-for-TSO replacement of the same type per approved data is generally not a major alteration. Form 337 documents major repairs and alterations — if the work does not meet that threshold, no Form 337 is required. The key qualifier is 'per approved data.'

preassessment.question: Replacing a TSO part with another TSO part of the same type, per approved data, is generally:
choices: A major alteration
A major repair
Not a major alteration
Preventive maintenance

questions.learningObjective: State the primary wiring standard for avionics installation in Part 145
questions.fullObjective: State that AC 43.13-1B or the aircraft manufacturer's wiring data is the most critical standard to follow when installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 environment.

flashcard.definition: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 (or the aircraft manufacturer's wiring data when it exists) is the primary standard for avionics wiring installation in a Part 145 shop, covering acceptable methods and practices.
flashcard.analogy: Like the NEC (National Electrical Code) for building wiring — the governing standard that defines what acceptable practice means.

jeopardy.question: When installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 shop, this advisory circular — or the aircraft manufacturer's wiring data — is the primary standard to follow.
jeopardy.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 covers aircraft electrical wiring acceptable methods. When manufacturer wiring data exists it takes precedence, but AC 43.13-1B is the primary fallback. AC 20-165B covers ADS-B. AC 91-78 covers EFBs. AC 43.13-2B covers aircraft structures.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most critical standard to follow when installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 environment?
choices: RTCA DO-160G environmental testing requirements
The STC holder's equipment installation manual exclusively
AC 43.13-1B or the aircraft manufacturer's wiring data
RTCA DO-178C software development assurance standards
multipleChoice.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 is the governing standard for aircraft wiring acceptable methods and practices. Manufacturer-specific wiring data takes precedence when it exists. DO-160G covers environmental testing. DO-178C covers software — neither addresses physical wiring practices.

practice.question: A technician routing wiring for an avionics installation is uncertain about minimum bend radius and bundle separation requirements. The correct source to consult is:
choices: AC 43.13-1B or the aircraft manufacturer's wiring data
The avionics unit's installation manual — it covers all wiring installation requirements
RTCA DO-160G — it defines all physical avionics installation standards
The FAA Flight Standards District Office for a written field approval
practice.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 is the reference for avionics wiring practices including bend radius, bundle separation, clamping, and routing. The equipment installation manual covers the unit's connections but not aircraft wiring practices. DO-160G covers environmental qualification, not installation technique.

preassessment.question: The primary standard for avionics wiring installation practices in a Part 145 shop is:
choices: RTCA DO-178C
RTCA DO-160G
14 CFR Part 65

questions.learningObjective: Verify test equipment calibration before use in a Part 145 shop
questions.fullObjective: Verify that specialized test equipment used in a Part 145 shop has a current calibration label and is within its calibration interval before use.

flashcard.definition: Before use in a Part 145 shop, specialized test equipment must have a current calibration label showing it is within its calibration interval. Out-of-calibration equipment produces invalid results and non-compliant records.
flashcard.analogy: Like verifying a thermometer against a known reference before using it in a critical process — if the tool is inaccurate, your measurements are meaningless.

jeopardy.question: Before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, a technician must verify that this is current and within its interval.
choices: The equipment's software version number
The shop's insurance certificate
The equipment's operating manual edition
The calibration label
jeopardy.explanation: All precision test equipment must have a current calibration label. Using out-of-calibration equipment invalidates measurements and may produce legally non-compliant maintenance records.

multipleChoice.question: Before using a pitot-static test set in a Part 145 shop, what must a technician verify?
choices: That the equipment has a current calibration label and is within its calibration interval
That the equipment has been used on the same aircraft type previously
That the equipment is specifically listed on the shop's capability list
That the software version meets the avionics manufacturer's minimum requirements
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 145 requires test equipment to be calibrated and within interval before use. Out-of-calibration equipment produces invalid readings and may create non-compliant maintenance records. Capability list, prior use, and software version are not the primary verification.

practice.question: You're about to perform a 91.413 transponder check. The IFR test set's calibration sticker shows a due date of last month. The correct action is:
choices: Do not use the equipment until it has been recalibrated and a current label applied
Proceed — one month expired is within acceptable tolerance for calibration intervals
Ask the IA whether the expired calibration is acceptable for this specific inspection
Use the equipment but note the expired calibration status in the maintenance record
practice.explanation: There is no regulatory tolerance for expired calibration — out-of-calibration means out-of-service. Using expired test equipment invalidates the transponder check. Documenting the expired status in the record does not make the work compliant. Recalibration is required before use.

preassessment.question: Before using a transponder test set in a Part 145 shop, you must verify it has a current:
choices: Software update
Calibration label
Work order number
FAA field approval

questions.learningObjective: List required elements of a maintenance log entry for an avionics repair
questions.fullObjective: List the required elements of a maintenance log entry for an avionics repair: description of work performed, parts used, date completed, technician name, certificate number, and return-to-service statement.

flashcard.definition: Required elements per §43.9: description of work performed, parts used, date completed, technician name, certificate number, and return-to-service statement.
flashcard.analogy: Like a contractor's invoice that doubles as a warranty record — documenting exactly what was done, what was installed, when, and by whom with their license number.

jeopardy.question: Of these log entry elements, this one is required by §43.9 but is most often omitted by new technicians.
choices: Certificate number
Date completed
Description of work
Return-to-service statement
jeopardy.explanation: The technician's certificate number is most frequently omitted — new techs include their name and signature but forget the certificate number, which §43.9 explicitly requires and which ties the entry to a specific FAA-certificated individual.

multipleChoice.question: Which list correctly states all required elements of a maintenance log entry for an avionics repair?
choices: Description of work performed, parts used, date completed, technician name, certificate number, and return-to-service statement
Description of work, aircraft serial number, date, and IA countersignature
Work order number, parts used, technician name, and shop authorization stamp
Description of work, date, parts used, and return-to-service signature only
multipleChoice.explanation: All six elements are required by §43.9. Aircraft serial number and IA countersignature are not required elements. Work order number and shop stamp are administrative tools, not regulatory requirements.

practice.question: A new tech writes: 'Installed GPS unit per STC. Date: 2026-02-22. J. Martinez.' What is missing to meet §43.9?
choices: The aircraft serial number and registration number
The certificate number and return-to-service statement
The work order number and shop supervisor's countersignature
The STC number and FAA approval reference letter
practice.explanation: The entry has description and date but is missing the technician's certificate number and return-to-service statement — both required by §43.9. STC numbers, aircraft serial numbers, and supervisor signatures are not §43.9 requirements.

preassessment.question: Which element is required in an avionics repair log entry per §43.9?
choices: Aircraft serial number
Work order number
Technician's certificate number
IA countersignature

questions.learningObjective: State that 91.413 requires transponder testing every 24 calendar months
questions.fullObjective: State that 14 CFR 91.413 requires transponder testing and inspection every 24 calendar months.

flashcard.definition: 14 CFR 91.413 requires transponder testing and inspection every 24 calendar months for aircraft operating in airspace where a transponder is required.
flashcard.analogy: Like a mandatory vehicle safety inspection every two years — required on a calendar cycle regardless of how much or how little the vehicle was used.

jeopardy.question: Per 14 CFR 91.413, transponder systems must be tested and inspected at this interval.
choices: Every 24 calendar months
Every 12 calendar months
Every 18 calendar months
Every 100 flight hours
jeopardy.explanation: 91.413 requires transponder testing every 24 calendar months — not 12 months or flight hours. The interval is calendar-based, running from the date of the last check regardless of hours flown.

multipleChoice.question: How often must transponder systems be tested and inspected per 14 CFR 91.413?
choices: Every 12 calendar months
Every 18 calendar months
Every 24 calendar months
Every 100 flight hours
multipleChoice.explanation: 91.413 requires transponder testing every 24 calendar months. A transponder checked in February 2024 is due by end of February 2026 regardless of how many hours the aircraft flew in between.

practice.question: An aircraft's logbook shows its last transponder check was in March 2024. It is now April 2026. Is the aircraft legal to operate in transponder-required airspace?
choices: Yes — transponder checks are only required for IFR operations, not VFR flight
Yes — the 24-month interval runs from the annual inspection date, not the transponder check date
No — the 24-month interval under 91.413 expired in March 2026
No — transponders must be re-checked every 12 months in Class B and C airspace
practice.explanation: 91.413 requires the check every 24 calendar months. March 2024 + 24 months = March 2026. Operating in April 2026 without a new check violates 91.413. The interval is independent of annual inspections and applies wherever a transponder is required.

preassessment.question: 14 CFR 91.413 requires transponder inspection every:
choices: 12 months
18 months
100 flight hours
24 calendar months

questions.learningObjective: Explain that Part 145 shop work is limited to its specific ratings and classes
questions.fullObjective: Explain that a Part 145 repair station's capabilities are limited to the specific ratings and classes listed in its operations specifications, and work outside those ratings is not permitted.

flashcard.definition: A Part 145 repair station may only perform work within its specific ratings and classes listed in its operations specifications. Work outside those ratings is not authorized regardless of individual technician qualifications.
flashcard.analogy: Like a contractor's license — a licensed electrician cannot perform plumbing work just because they are in the same building.

jeopardy.question: A Part 145 repair station's authorized scope of work is defined and limited by these, listed in its operations specifications.
choices: Its FAA-approved drug testing program
Its ratings and classes in the Ops Specs
The individual technicians' A&P certificates
Its ISO 9001 quality management certification
jeopardy.explanation: Operations specifications define what a Part 145 station is authorized to do. Individual A&P certificates authorize the individual technician, not the shop organization. ISO certification is a quality management standard, not an FAA authorization.

multipleChoice.question: A Part 145 shop receives work for an aircraft component outside its current ratings. What is the correct action?
choices: Perform the work if a certificated technician on staff holds the relevant A&P endorsement
Perform the work and document it under the closest applicable rating
Decline the work — the shop may only perform work within its specific ratings and classes
Accept the work and request an emergency rating expansion from the local FSDO
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 145 limits a shop to work within its Ops Specs ratings and classes. An individual tech's A&P certificate does not extend the shop's authorization. There is no emergency rating expansion process.

practice.question: Your Part 145 shop holds avionics instrument and communications ratings. A customer brings in a landing gear actuation component for repair. Can your shop legally perform this work?
choices: No — landing gear actuation falls outside your shop's ratings and is not authorized
Yes — Part 145 shops may perform any maintenance as long as a qualified technician is available
Yes — electronic landing gear controls overlap with your avionics instrument rating
No — only FAA-designated airframe shops are authorized to work on any aircraft component
practice.explanation: Avionics instrument and communications ratings do not extend to airframe systems like landing gear actuation. The Ops Specs define the boundary. Performing work outside those ratings is a Part 145 violation regardless of individual technician qualifications.

preassessment.question: A Part 145 shop's authorized scope of work is defined by:
choices: Individual technician A&P certificates
Its ISO quality certification
The local FSDO's annual renewal letter
Its ratings and classes in its Ops Specs

questions.learningObjective: State the specific regulation requiring periodic transponder testing
questions.fullObjective: State that 14 CFR 91.413 is the specific regulation requiring periodic testing and inspection of transponder systems.

flashcard.definition: The specific FAA regulation requiring periodic testing and inspection of transponder systems — every 24 calendar months.
flashcard.analogy: If 91.411 is the altimeter's biennial check requirement, 91.413 is the transponder's — parallel structure for two different systems.

jeopardy.question: This is the specific regulation number requiring periodic testing and inspection of transponder systems.
choices: 14 CFR Part 43
jeopardy.explanation: 91.413 is specifically for transponders. 91.411 covers altimeter and pitot-static systems. 91.207 covers ELTs. Part 43 covers maintenance standards generally.

multipleChoice.question: A customer asks which FAA regulation requires their transponder to be periodically tested. The correct answer is:
choices: 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix E
multipleChoice.explanation: 91.413 is the transponder-specific regulation. 91.411 covers altimeter/pitot-static systems. Appendix E to Part 43 details test procedures but 91.413 is the mandate. 91.207 covers ELTs.

practice.question: You're training a new tech on required avionics inspections. They ask which regulation number covers the mandatory transponder inspection cycle. You tell them:
choices: 14 CFR 91.411 — it covers all periodic avionics inspection requirements in one place
14 CFR 91.413 — the regulation specifically requiring transponder testing and inspection
14 CFR Part 43 — it covers all maintenance requirements including transponder checks
14 CFR 91.207 — it covers all periodic avionics testing requirements
practice.explanation: 91.413 is the regulation exclusively addressing transponder testing requirements. 91.411 is for altimeter/pitot-static systems. Part 43 sets maintenance performance standards broadly. 91.207 covers ELTs. Each system has its own regulation.

preassessment.question: The regulation specifically requiring transponder testing is:
choices: 14 CFR Part 43

questions.learningObjective: State the definition of an STC
questions.fullObjective: State that an STC (Supplemental Type Certificate) is an FAA approval that permits modification of a type-certificated aircraft in a manner not covered by the original type certificate.

flashcard.definition: An FAA approval that permits modification of a type-certificated aircraft in a manner not covered by the original type certificate. The STC holder owns the approved data package for that specific modification.
flashcard.analogy: Like an officially approved aftermarket upgrade — sanctioned by the certifying authority, covering exactly the modification specified and no more.

jeopardy.question: This FAA approval permits modifying a type-certificated aircraft in a way not covered by the original type certificate.
choices: Airworthiness Directive
Supplemental Type Certificate
FAA Form 337
Parts Manufacturer Approval
jeopardy.explanation: An STC is the FAA approval for modifications beyond the original type certificate. An AD mandates a safety change. Form 337 documents a major repair or alteration. A PMA approves a replacement part. Only an STC authorizes the modification itself.

multipleChoice.question: What is an STC?
choices: A required FAA inspection interval for modified aircraft systems
An FAA approval authorizing modification of a type-certificated aircraft beyond its original type certificate
A document recording the completion of a major repair on an aircraft
A manufacturer's approval for using a parts substitute on a certificated aircraft
multipleChoice.explanation: An STC is an FAA approval authorizing a specific modification to a specific aircraft type, including the approved data package. ADs mandate action. Form 337 documents work. PMA approves parts. Only an STC authorizes the original modification.

practice.question: A shop receives an avionics unit with an STC data package for installation on a specific aircraft type. What does the STC mean for this installation?
choices: The unit is approved for installation on any aircraft with similar avionics architecture
The STC replaces the need for a maintenance record entry for this installation
The shop must obtain additional FAA approval before installing since the STC modifies the type certificate
The FAA has approved this specific modification for this aircraft type and the installation must follow the STC data
practice.explanation: The STC is the FAA approval for this specific modification on this aircraft type. The installation must follow the STC data package to be legal. STCs are aircraft-type specific. The STC itself is the required approval — no additional approval is needed to proceed.

preassessment.question: An STC is an FAA approval that:
choices: Permits modifying an aircraft beyond its original type certificate
Mandates a safety inspection interval
Documents a major repair
Approves a parts substitute only

questions.learningObjective: State who is responsible for ensuring current technical data availability under Part 145
questions.fullObjective: State that under Part 145, the repair station's Quality Control department and accountable manager are responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance.

flashcard.definition: Under Part 145, the Quality Control department and accountable manager are responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance.
flashcard.analogy: Like a hospital's compliance officer and medical director being jointly responsible that all procedures use current protocols — systemic responsibility, not just individual practitioner responsibility.

jeopardy.question: Under Part 145, these two roles share responsibility for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance.
choices: The IA and shop foreman
The Quality Control department and accountable manager
The FAA FSDO and repair station owner
The technician and their direct supervisor
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 places responsibility for current technical data on the Quality Control system and the accountable manager. QC ensures data is current and available. The accountable manager is the person the FAA holds responsible for the station's overall compliance.

multipleChoice.question: Under Part 145, who is responsible for ensuring that current technical data is available and used during maintenance?
choices: Each individual technician performing the specific work
The FAA inspector assigned to conduct surveillance of the station
The Quality Control department and accountable manager
The aircraft owner or operator who brought the work in
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 145 places systemic responsibility for current technical data on the QC department and accountable manager. While individual technicians must use current data, the organizational responsibility for ensuring it is provided and maintained belongs to QC and the accountable manager.

practice.question: A technician finds the maintenance manual on the shop floor is two revisions old. Under Part 145, who bears organizational responsibility for this lapse?
choices: The Quality Control department and accountable manager
The individual technician who should have checked before starting
The aircraft manufacturer for not notifying the shop of revisions
The FSDO oversight inspector who approved the station's manual control system
practice.explanation: While technicians must use current data, the organizational responsibility for ensuring current tech data is available rests with QC and the accountable manager under Part 145. The FSDO oversees compliance but is not responsible for the station's internal data management.

preassessment.question: Under Part 145, organizational responsibility for ensuring current technical data is available rests with:
choices: Individual technicians
The FAA FSDO
The aircraft owner
The QC department and accountable manager

questions.learningObjective: Explain that preventive maintenance under Part 43 is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate
questions.fullObjective: Explain that under 14 CFR Part 43, preventive maintenance is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate and is restricted to simple tasks listed in the regulation.

flashcard.definition: Under 14 CFR Part 43, preventive maintenance is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate — restricted to simple tasks explicitly listed in Part 43 Appendix A.
flashcard.analogy: Like a homeowner being allowed to replace their own light switches but not wire a subpanel — a defined, limited scope for non-professionals on their own property.

jeopardy.question: Under 14 CFR Part 43, this type of maintenance is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate, for simple listed tasks only.
choices: Preventive maintenance
Major maintenance
Conditional maintenance
Routine inspection
jeopardy.explanation: Preventive maintenance is the only maintenance a certificated pilot (not a mechanic) may legally perform, and only on aircraft they own or operate — limited to simple tasks listed in Part 43 Appendix A.

multipleChoice.question: Why is preventive maintenance under Part 43 limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate?
choices: Because pilots receive basic maintenance training and can perform any simple task
Because Part 43 restricts all maintenance to aircraft owners regardless of certification level
Because it covers only simple tasks not requiring mechanic training, on the owner's aircraft only
Because the FAA determined pilots are more reliable than mechanics for owner-performed tasks
multipleChoice.explanation: Preventive maintenance covers simple tasks (oil changes, tire replacement, bulb changes) that don't require mechanic-level training. The owner/operator limitation ensures the person has a direct stake in the aircraft's airworthiness. Performing preventive maintenance on someone else's aircraft is not authorized.

practice.question: A certificated private pilot wants to replace a position light bulb on an aircraft they rent from a flight school. Are they authorized under Part 43?
choices: Yes — replacing bulbs is preventive maintenance any certificated pilot can perform
Yes — the flight school can specifically authorize pilots to perform simple preventive tasks
No — preventive maintenance is limited to pilots on aircraft they own or operate, not rental aircraft
No — only A&P mechanics may replace any aircraft electrical lighting components
practice.explanation: Part 43 limits preventive maintenance to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate — not aircraft they rent. 'Operate' has a specific regulatory meaning here (registered operator), not a temporary renter. The rental aircraft belongs to the flight school.

preassessment.question: Under Part 43, preventive maintenance may only be performed by:
choices: Any FAA-certificated person on any aircraft
A&P mechanics on owner-operated aircraft only
Certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate
Repair stations on any certificated aircraft

questions.learningObjective: State the typical recurrent training interval in Part 145 station manuals
questions.fullObjective: State that most Part 145 repair station manuals require recurrent training for personnel at least every 24 months.

flashcard.definition: Most Part 145 repair station manuals require recurrent training for maintenance personnel at least every 24 months.
flashcard.analogy: Like CPR recertification on a fixed cycle — required to ensure skills and knowledge stay current with evolving standards.

jeopardy.question: Most Part 145 repair station manuals require personnel to complete recurrent training at least this often.
choices: Every 12 months
Every 18 months
Every 24 months
Every 36 months
jeopardy.explanation: The industry standard in Part 145 repair station manuals is recurrent training every 24 months — aligning with other 24-month aviation maintenance cycles. The specific interval is set in each station's manual; 24 months is the standard most stations use.

multipleChoice.question: How often do most Part 145 repair station manuals require recurrent training for maintenance personnel?
choices: Every 24 months
Every 6 months
Every 12 months
Upon each new aircraft type added to the capability list
multipleChoice.explanation: Most Part 145 station manuals specify recurrent training at least every 24 months. The FAA requires a training program under Part 145.163, but the specific interval is set in the station's own manual — 24 months is the industry standard.

practice.question: A tech completed their last recurrent training in January 2024. It is now March 2026. Are they current per most Part 145 repair station manuals?
choices: Yes — recurrent training is every 36 months under the standard so time remains
Yes — recurrent training applies only to new hires in their first three years
No — the standard 24-month interval expired in January 2026
No — recurrent training must be completed annually under Part 145.163
practice.explanation: January 2024 + 24 months = January 2026. By March 2026 the standard 24-month interval has expired. The tech must complete recurrent training to remain current.

preassessment.question: Most Part 145 stations require recurrent training for personnel every:
choices: 6 months
12 months
36 months
24 months

questions.learningObjective: State that Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment
questions.fullObjective: State that Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment and that appropriate non-conductive extinguishing agents must be used.

flashcard.definition: A Class C fire involves energized electrical equipment. Non-conductive extinguishing agents (CO₂, dry chemical) must be used — never water, which conducts electricity and creates an electrocution hazard.
flashcard.analogy: Like the appliance warning — unplug before using water near it. With Class C fires, de-energizing first is often not possible.

jeopardy.question: This class of fire involves energized electrical equipment and requires non-conductive extinguishing agents.
choices: Class C
Class A
Class B
Class D
jeopardy.explanation: Class C = energized electrical equipment. Class A = ordinary combustibles. Class B = flammable liquids and gases. Class D = combustible metals. Using water on a Class C fire creates an electrocution hazard.

multipleChoice.question: A soldering station in the avionics shop catches fire while energized. What type of fire is this and what agent is appropriate?
choices: Class A fire — water or foam extinguisher is appropriate
Class B fire — CO₂ required, keep away from electrical panels
Class D fire — use dry sand or a specialized Class D agent
Class C fire — CO₂ or dry chemical non-conductive agent required
multipleChoice.explanation: An energized electrical fire is Class C. CO₂ or dry chemical extinguishers are appropriate — both are non-conductive. Using water creates an electrocution hazard. If the equipment can be safely de-energized first, the fire may downgrade to Class A, but de-energizing must happen before any water application.

practice.question: You need to extinguish a small fire at an energized test bench. Which action is correct?
choices: Use CO₂ or dry chemical — the energized equipment makes this a Class C fire requiring non-conductive agents
Use a water extinguisher — it is most effective for small shop fires of any type
Use a foam extinguisher — it is safe for all fire classes in an avionics shop
Wait for the circuit breaker to trip automatically and de-energize before taking action
practice.explanation: An energized electrical fire is Class C, requiring non-conductive extinguishing agents. Water conducts electricity and creates an electrocution hazard. Waiting for automatic circuit protection is not a safe response to an active fire.

preassessment.question: A Class C fire involves:
choices: Flammable liquids
Combustible metals
Ordinary combustibles
Energized electrical equipment

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose a stuck microphone as the cause of a constant tone on a comm frequency
questions.fullObjective: Given a pilot hearing a constant tone instead of an ATC response, diagnose a stuck microphone transmitting continuously and blocking the frequency as the most likely cause.

flashcard.definition: A constant tone on a comm frequency instead of ATC voice responses indicates a stuck PTT — a microphone transmitting continuously and blocking the frequency for all users.
flashcard.analogy: Like someone holding down a walkie-talkie transmit button — nobody else can get through until that button is released.

jeopardy.question: A pilot hears a constant tone on the frequency instead of ATC responses. This is the most likely diagnosis.
choices: A stuck microphone transmitting continuously
A failed ATC ground transmitter
A receiver squelch circuit failure
ILS localizer signal interference on the comm frequency
jeopardy.explanation: A constant tone replacing all voice traffic is the classic stuck PTT symptom. Someone's microphone is transmitting continuously and blocking the frequency. Squelch failure causes noise, not a tone. ATC transmitter failure causes silence. ILS bleed-over affects specific frequencies differently.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot reports hearing only a constant tone on the ATC frequency — no voice responses at all. The most likely diagnosis is:
choices: The ATC facility's ground transmitter has failed
The aircraft's receiver squelch control is adjusted too tight
A stuck microphone on the frequency is blocking all transmissions
The COM radio is receiving on the wrong sideband mode
multipleChoice.explanation: A constant tone replacing all voice traffic is the classic stuck mic symptom. Squelch failure causes noise or silence, not a tone. ATC transmitter failure causes silence. Wrong sideband causes garbled audio, not a pure blocking tone.

practice.question: During a run-up, a pilot reports hearing a constant tone on 121.5 MHz but no ATC voice. An avionics tech overhears this. What should the tech consider first?
choices: A stuck PTT — either on this aircraft or another aircraft using the same frequency
The aircraft's COM receiver has failed internally and needs immediate bench testing
The pilot's headset microphone is shorted to ground and must be replaced immediately
The audio panel has failed and is generating an internal 1000 Hz test tone
practice.explanation: A constant tone on a frequency is the signature of a stuck PTT. Importantly, it may not be this aircraft's mic — any aircraft with a stuck PTT on that frequency would produce this symptom. The tech should check whether the aircraft's own PTT is stuck before assuming an internal receiver fault.

preassessment.question: A constant tone instead of ATC voice on the comm frequency most likely indicates:
choices: A failed squelch circuit
A receiver antenna fault
A stuck microphone transmitting continuously
An ILS signal interference source

questions.learningObjective: Confirm a parasitic draw source using ammeter in series
questions.fullObjective: Confirm a faulty switch as the source of a parasitic draw by using an ammeter in series with the circuit and activating the suspect switch while monitoring current.

flashcard.definition: To confirm a faulty switch as a parasitic draw source, place an ammeter in series with the circuit and activate the suspect switch while monitoring current. A current increase confirms the switch is the source.
flashcard.analogy: Like checking which faucet is draining your water pressure — turn them on one at a time while watching the flow meter.

jeopardy.question: To confirm a switch is causing a parasitic draw, you place this instrument in series with the circuit and activate the switch.
choices: A voltmeter
An ohmmeter
An ammeter
A wattmeter
jeopardy.explanation: Parasitic draw is current — measured with an ammeter in series. A voltmeter measures voltage in parallel. An ohmmeter measures resistance with power off. A wattmeter measures power but is not the standard tool for parasitic draw diagnosis.

multipleChoice.question: How do you confirm a faulty switch is the source of a parasitic draw in an aircraft circuit?
