choices: Place an ammeter in series with the circuit and activate the suspect switch while monitoring current
Measure voltage across the switch terminals with the circuit energized
Measure resistance across the switch with the circuit de-energized and power off
Pull the circuit breaker and check if the battery drain stops to isolate the branch
multipleChoice.explanation: Parasitic draw is current, so an ammeter in series is required. Activating the suspect switch while watching current directly confirms whether that switch is the draw source. Voltage measurement identifies a drop, not draw. Pulling the breaker isolates the circuit but does not identify the specific faulty component.

practice.question: A tech suspects a landing light switch is causing a 200mA parasitic draw. The correct procedure to confirm this is:
choices: Measure voltage at the switch terminals to verify the switch is receiving power correctly
Install an ammeter in series with the landing light circuit and operate the switch while monitoring current
Replace the switch as a first step and verify the drain disappears to confirm the diagnosis
Use an ohmmeter across the switch terminals to check for internal resistance leakage at rest
practice.explanation: An ammeter in series with the circuit, with the suspect switch activated, directly confirms whether that switch is the current draw source. Voltage measurement shows potential but not draw. Replacing the switch without confirmation is shotgun troubleshooting. An ohmmeter requires de-energized conditions and measures static resistance, not live parasitic current.

preassessment.question: To confirm a parasitic draw from a suspect switch, use an ammeter placed:
choices: Across the switch terminals
Across the battery terminals
In parallel with the fuse
In series with the circuit

questions.learningObjective: State that an SDS must be available for each hazardous material in a Part 145 shop
questions.fullObjective: State that the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is the document that must be readily available for each hazardous material used in a Part 145 avionics shop.

flashcard.definition: The document required to be readily available for each hazardous material used in a Part 145 avionics shop — covering hazards, PPE requirements, first aid, and disposal procedures.
flashcard.analogy: Like a medication package insert required by law — it must be on hand and tells you exactly what you are working with and what to do if something goes wrong.

jeopardy.question: This document must be readily available for each hazardous material used in a Part 145 avionics shop.
choices: The FAA hazmat approval letter
The manufacturer's safety bulletin
The Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
The shop's liability insurance policy
jeopardy.explanation: OSHA requires Safety Data Sheets (SDS) to be readily accessible for all hazardous materials in the workplace. In an avionics shop, this includes solvents, adhesives, coatings, and cleaning agents.

multipleChoice.question: What document must be readily available for each hazardous material used in a Part 145 avionics shop?
choices: The product's manufacturer warranty documentation
The FAA type certificate for the specific material
The shop's hazmat storage insurance documentation
The Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
multipleChoice.explanation: OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard requires SDS documents to be readily accessible for all hazardous chemicals in the workplace. This applies to solvents, conformal coatings, flux, and other chemicals. No FAA type certificate exists for chemicals.

practice.question: A technician is about to use a solvent cleaner on a connector assembly. Before starting, what document should they be able to locate quickly if needed?
choices: The Safety Data Sheet for the solvent being used
The connector's manufacturer installation instructions
The repair station's capability list for chemical approvals
The aircraft maintenance manual covering connector cleaning procedures
practice.explanation: OSHA requires the SDS to be readily available for any hazardous material in use. It contains safety hazards, required PPE, first aid procedures, and disposal information. Before using any chemical, the SDS must be readily accessible — not just somewhere in the facility.

preassessment.question: The document required to be readily available for each hazardous material in a Part 145 shop is the:
choices: FAA hazmat authorization letter
Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
Material approval certificate
Shop safety operations manual

questions.learningObjective: Explain why hearing protection is critical in a busy avionics shop
questions.fullObjective: Explain why hearing protection is critical in a busy avionics shop: cumulative noise-induced hearing loss is a long-term hazard from sustained exposure to engine run-up and shop noise.

flashcard.definition: Hearing protection is critical in an avionics shop because noise-induced hearing loss is cumulative and permanent — each unprotected exposure adds to total damage, even when individual exposures seem tolerable.
flashcard.analogy: Like sunscreen for your ears — you do not feel the damage happening, but it accumulates with every unprotected exposure.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary reason hearing protection is critical in a busy avionics shop with engine run-up activity nearby.
choices: Short-term hearing loss affects radio communication quality during operations
OSHA requires it only for insurance liability compliance purposes
Sustained exposure causes cumulative permanent noise-induced hearing loss
Engine noise creates EMI that interferes with electronic test equipment
jeopardy.explanation: Noise-induced hearing loss is cumulative and permanent — each unprotected exposure contributes to total damage even if no single event seems severe. It is a long-term occupational hazard, not just an acute discomfort issue.

multipleChoice.question: Why is hearing protection critical in an avionics shop with regular engine run-up activity nearby?
choices: Cumulative noise-induced hearing loss from repeated exposure is a long-term permanent hazard
Engine noise at run-up levels can cause immediate permanent deafness from a single exposure
OSHA requires it specifically for Part 145 shops within 500 feet of active runways
Hearing protection prevents radio frequency interference with sensitive avionics test equipment
multipleChoice.explanation: The key word is cumulative — hearing damage builds over time. A single run-up may not seem dangerous, but repeated unprotected exposure leads to permanent hearing loss. This is why hearing protection is required even when individual noise levels seem tolerable.

practice.question: A new tech says 'I only skip hearing protection during quick run-ups — it doesn't seem that loud.' How should you respond?
choices: Explain that noise-induced hearing loss is cumulative and permanent — every unprotected exposure adds to total damage regardless of duration
Agree — occasional short exposures are below OSHA action levels and safe to skip without protection
Tell them the real concern is radio interference with avionics equipment, not hearing damage
Explain the rule only applies to sustained exposures above 90 dB for more than 8 continuous hours
practice.explanation: Noise-induced hearing loss accumulates with each unprotected exposure — there is no safe 'occasional skip.' The damage is permanent and does not announce itself until significant loss has already occurred. Hearing protection during every run-up and high-noise activity is the only way to prevent cumulative damage.

preassessment.question: The primary hazard of repeated unprotected exposure to engine run-up noise is:
choices: Temporary tinnitus that resolves within 24 hours
Radio communication interference
Short-term balance and dizziness issues
Cumulative permanent hearing loss

questions.learningObjective: GPS requires 4 satellites for 3D nav with WAAS
questions.fullObjective: State that a GPS receiver must track at least 4 satellites to compute 3D navigation with WAAS, providing both horizontal and vertical position accuracy.

flashcard.definition: A GPS receiver needs at least 4 satellites to compute 3D position (latitude, longitude, and altitude). WAAS augments this for precision approach accuracy — horizontal and vertical.
flashcard.analogy: Three satellites give you a flat X/Y fix; the fourth satellite adds altitude — like a fourth wall that completes a box.

jeopardy.question: A GPS receiver must track at least this many satellites to compute 3D navigation with WAAS precision.
choices: 4 satellites
3 satellites
2 satellites
6 satellites
jeopardy.explanation: Three satellites provide 2D position; a fourth satellite allows the receiver to solve for altitude as well, enabling 3D navigation. WAAS uses ground correction stations to improve accuracy for both axes.

multipleChoice.question: How many satellites must a GPS receiver track to compute 3D navigation with WAAS?
multipleChoice.explanation: Four satellites are the minimum for 3D GPS: three to solve the 2D position and one additional to solve for altitude. WAAS provides ground-based differential correction to improve the accuracy of that 3D fix.

practice.question: A tech is troubleshooting a GPS that shows a 2D position fix but no altitude. What is the most likely cause?
choices: WAAS signal has been lost
The GPS antenna is faulty
The receiver is only tracking 3 satellites instead of the 4 needed for 3D nav
The altimeter encoder is not connected to the GPS
practice.explanation: A 2D fix without altitude means the GPS is solving only the horizontal plane — typically because only 3 satellites are visible. The 4th satellite is required to solve the altitude dimension for a 3D position fix.

preassessment.question: How many satellites does GPS need for 3D navigation with WAAS?

questions.learningObjective: VOR antenna type and frequency range
questions.fullObjective: State that VOR antennas are typically V-shaped or bent-dipole antennas tuned to the 108 to 117.95 MHz frequency range and mounted on the aircraft's vertical stabilizer or fuselage.

flashcard.definition: VOR antennas are V-shaped or bent-dipole antennas tuned to 108–117.95 MHz. Typically mounted on the vertical stabilizer or fuselage. They receive VHF omnidirectional range signals from ground stations.
flashcard.analogy: Like a TV rabbit ear antenna — V-shaped, tuned to specific VHF frequencies to pick up the right signal.

jeopardy.question: These V-shaped or bent-dipole antennas tuned to 108–117.95 MHz receive signals from VOR ground stations.
choices: GPS patch antennas
VOR antennas
DME blade antennas
Glideslope antennas
jeopardy.explanation: VOR antennas are V-shaped or bent-dipole designs, mounted on the vertical stabilizer or fuselage, tuned to the VOR frequency range of 108.0–117.95 MHz (shared with ILS localizer on 108.1–111.95 MHz odd decimals).

multipleChoice.question: What type of antenna is used for VOR reception and what frequency range does it cover?
choices: Blade antenna, 329–335 MHz
Loop antenna, 190–535 kHz
V-shaped or bent-dipole, 108–117.95 MHz
Patch antenna, 1575.42 MHz
multipleChoice.explanation: VOR uses V-shaped or bent-dipole antennas in the 108–117.95 MHz VHF range. Loop antennas are for ADF/NDB; patch antennas are for GPS; glideslope operates at ~329 MHz.

practice.question: During a VOR system inspection, a tech is verifying the antenna. Which antenna description and location would be correct for a standard installation?
choices: A small blade antenna on the belly, 329 MHz
A loop antenna under the fuselage, 190–535 kHz
A GPS patch antenna flush-mounted on the glare shield
A V-shaped or bent-dipole on the vertical stabilizer or fuselage, 108–117.95 MHz
practice.explanation: The standard VOR antenna is a V-shaped or bent-dipole design mounted on the vertical stabilizer or fuselage, tuned to 108–117.95 MHz. Loop antennas are ADF/NDB; blades at 329 MHz are glideslope; patch antennas are GPS.

preassessment.question: What type is a VOR antenna and what frequency range?
choices: V-shaped/bent-dipole, 108–117.95 MHz
Blade, 329 MHz
Loop, 190–535 kHz
Patch, 1575 MHz

questions.learningObjective: DME measures slant-range distance in nautical miles
questions.fullObjective: Explain that DME provides pilots with slant-range distance to the DME ground station in nautical miles by measuring pulse round-trip time.

flashcard.definition: DME measures slant-range distance to a ground station in nautical miles by measuring the time for a pulse to travel to the station and return. Slant range includes the diagonal — not purely horizontal distance.
flashcard.analogy: Like sonar on a submarine — send a ping, time the echo, calculate distance.

jeopardy.question: This navigation system provides slant-range distance to a ground station in nautical miles by measuring pulse round-trip time.
choices: VOR
ILS
DME
ADF
jeopardy.explanation: DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) interrogates a ground station; the station replies; the receiver measures round-trip time and converts to nautical miles. The result is slant range — the diagonal distance, not horizontal ground distance.

multipleChoice.question: How does DME determine distance to a ground station?
choices: By measuring the phase difference between two signals
By receiving a continuous CW signal and measuring signal strength
By counting the number of VOR radials between two waypoints
By measuring the round-trip time of a pulse sent to and replied by the station
multipleChoice.explanation: DME is a pulse-echo system: the airborne unit interrogates the ground station, which replies after a fixed delay; the receiver measures total elapsed time, subtracts the known reply delay, and computes slant-range distance.

practice.question: At 10,000 feet AGL, directly over a DME station, the receiver displays 1.6 NM. Why doesn't it show 0.0 NM?
choices: DME measures slant range — the diagonal from aircraft to station, which includes altitude
The DME receiver has a minimum range filter of 1.5 NM
The station is 1.6 NM offset from directly below the aircraft
DME accuracy degrades within 5 NM — the reading is an error
practice.explanation: DME measures slant range — the straight-line distance from aircraft to station. At 10,000 ft AGL directly overhead, you're about 1.6 NM slant range even though horizontal distance is zero. Slant range and ground range converge at distances much greater than your altitude.

preassessment.question: What does DME measure?
choices: Horizontal ground distance only
Slant-range distance in nautical miles via pulse round-trip time
Vertical altitude above the station
Bearing TO the station

questions.learningObjective: Marker beacon provides tones and lights at ILS fixed points
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the Marker Beacon provides pilots with coded audio tones and cockpit light indications at fixed distance points along an ILS approach.

flashcard.definition: Marker beacons transmit 75 MHz vertical fan-shaped signals at fixed points along an ILS approach. Outer marker: slow dashes, blue light. Middle marker: alternating dots/dashes, amber light. Inner marker: fast dots, white light.
flashcard.analogy: Like mile markers on a highway — fixed reference points that tell you exactly where you are on the approach.

jeopardy.question: Coded audio tones and blue, amber, or white cockpit lights at fixed distance points on an ILS approach are provided by this system.
choices: DME arc alerts
GPWS terrain warnings
TCAS proximity alerts
Marker beacon system
jeopardy.explanation: Marker beacons transmit at 75 MHz vertically. The outer marker (blue light, slow dashes) is ~4–7 NM from threshold; middle marker (amber, alternating dot-dash) is ~3,500 ft; inner marker (white, fast dots) is at the decision altitude point.

multipleChoice.question: What does the marker beacon system provide during an ILS approach?
choices: Coded audio tones and light indications at fixed distance points on the approach
Vertical glidepath guidance at 3 degrees
Lateral course deviation guidance to the runway centerline
Wind shear warnings based on airspeed fluctuations
multipleChoice.explanation: Marker beacons are position-fix systems only — they tell you WHERE you are on the ILS, not how to correct your course. Localizer provides lateral guidance; glideslope provides vertical guidance. Markers add situational awareness at known distance points.

practice.question: During a simulated ILS approach, the student hears rapid dots and sees a white light illuminate. What does this indicate?
choices: Outer marker — approximately 4–7 NM from threshold
Inner marker — at or near the instrument decision altitude
Middle marker — approximately 3,500 ft from threshold
Glideslope intercept — the aircraft has captured the glidepath
practice.explanation: Rapid dots + white light = inner marker. This indicates the aircraft is passing the innermost marker, typically co-located with the ILS decision altitude (DA) point. Outer marker is slow dashes/blue; middle marker is alternating dot-dash/amber.

preassessment.question: What does the outer marker beacon indicate?
choices: Inner marker light activates
Glideslope intercept point
Fixed distance point with coded tone and blue cockpit light
Runway touchdown zone

questions.learningObjective: OBS selects desired VOR radial
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the Omnibearing Selector (OBS) on a VOR indicator allows the pilot to select and track a desired VOR radial for navigation.

flashcard.definition: The OBS knob on a VOR CDI/HSI allows the pilot to manually select a desired VOR radial (0–360°) for course tracking. Rotating the OBS centers the CDI needle when the aircraft is on that radial, providing TO/FROM and course deviation information.
flashcard.analogy: Like adjusting a compass rose — you dial in the road you want to drive, and the needle shows whether you're on it.

jeopardy.question: On a VOR indicator, this control allows the pilot to select a desired radial for course tracking.
choices: The Omnibearing Selector (OBS)
The autopilot heading bug
The Course Deviation Indicator needle
The HSI bearing pointer
jeopardy.explanation: The OBS (Omnibearing Selector) rotates the course card to select a desired VOR radial. When the CDI needle centers with the OBS set to a given radial, the aircraft is on that radial — TO or FROM the station as shown by the flag.

multipleChoice.question: What is the function of the Omnibearing Selector (OBS) on a VOR receiver indicator?
choices: It adjusts the receiver squelch threshold for VOR audio
It allows the pilot to select and track a desired VOR radial
It sets the VOR station frequency to receive
It controls the bearing pointer on the RMI
multipleChoice.explanation: The OBS sets the selected course (radial) on the CDI display. When the OBS is set to the desired radial and the CDI needle centers, the aircraft is tracking on that radial. The TO/FROM flag indicates whether the radial leads toward or away from the station.

practice.question: A student pilot has tuned VOR 115.0 and wants to fly inbound on the 180° radial. What should they do with the OBS?
choices: Set OBS to 180° — this selects the radial FROM the station
Set OBS to 090° — halfway between north and south
Set OBS to 360° — inbound on the 180° radial means flying FROM south TO north, so select the reciprocal
The OBS only works for ILS — leave it at 0°
practice.explanation: Flying INBOUND on the 180° radial means flying FROM south TO the station (northbound). The inbound course is the RECIPROCAL of the radial — 360° (or 000°). Set OBS to 360° and the needle will center with a TO flag as you fly toward the station on that course.

preassessment.question: What does the OBS control on a VOR indicator do?
choices: Adjusts squelch
Controls bearing pointer
Tunes VOR frequency
Selects desired VOR radial for tracking

questions.learningObjective: WAAS is FAA ground-based correction for GPS precision approaches
questions.fullObjective: State that WAAS (Wide Area Augmentation System) is an FAA ground-based correction system that improves GPS accuracy for precision approach guidance.

flashcard.definition: An FAA ground-based correction system that broadcasts GPS correction data via geostationary satellites, improving GPS accuracy to approximately 1 meter horizontally and 1.5 meters vertically — sufficient for LPV precision approach guidance.
flashcard.analogy: Like a spell-checker for GPS — it identifies and corrects signal errors in real time, making the answer more precise.

jeopardy.question: This FAA ground-based correction system improves GPS accuracy to enable precision approach guidance (LPV) in the United States.
choices: RAIM
WAAS
TCAS
FMS
jeopardy.explanation: WAAS (Wide Area Augmentation System) uses a network of ground reference stations to measure GPS errors and broadcast corrections via geostationary satellites. This improves accuracy to ~1m, enabling LPV (Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance) approaches.

multipleChoice.question: What is WAAS and what does it do for GPS navigation?
choices: An airborne sensor that detects satellite failures and alerts the crew
A military encryption system that restricts GPS accuracy for civilian use
A ground-based correction system that improves GPS accuracy for precision approach guidance
A backup navigation system that operates independently of GPS satellites
multipleChoice.explanation: WAAS is an FAA ground network (not airborne) that corrects GPS signal errors. The corrections are uplinked to geostationary satellites and broadcast to suitably equipped aircraft, enabling sub-meter accuracy for LPV and other precision RNAV approaches.

practice.question: A pilot planning an LPV approach asks why WAAS is required for vertical guidance. What should the avionics tech explain?
choices: WAAS provides the glideslope signal that GPS alone cannot generate
LPV approaches use ILS glideslope; WAAS just verifies GPS position
WAAS encrypts GPS data to prevent spoofing during approaches
Standard GPS accuracy isn't precise enough for vertical guidance — WAAS corrections improve it to the ~1m accuracy needed for LPV approaches
practice.explanation: Standard GPS has horizontal accuracy of ~5m and vertical accuracy of ~10m — not precise enough for precision approach vertical guidance. WAAS corrections bring accuracy to ~1m horizontal and ~1.5m vertical, which is sufficient for LPV approaches with decision altitudes as low as 200 ft AGL.

preassessment.question: What does WAAS do?
choices: Ground-based correction system improving GPS accuracy for precision approaches
Detects satellite failures
Military GPS encryption
Independent backup navigation

questions.learningObjective: Lightning detection systems detect and display storm bearing and intensity
questions.fullObjective: Explain that lightning detection systems in aircraft detect electromagnetic discharges from storm cells and display their bearing and relative intensity to the crew for weather avoidance.

flashcard.definition: Aircraft lightning detectors sense the electromagnetic discharge of lightning and display storm cell bearing and relative intensity on a cockpit display. They help crews identify and avoid areas of convective activity — particularly useful at night or in IMC.
flashcard.analogy: Like a seismic sensor that maps earthquake activity — it shows where lightning is happening so you can route around it.

jeopardy.question: These airborne systems detect electromagnetic discharges from storm cells and display bearing and relative intensity on a cockpit display for weather avoidance.
choices: Traffic collision avoidance systems
Radio altimeters
Lightning detection systems
GPWS terrain warning systems
jeopardy.explanation: Lightning detection systems (e.g., Stormscope, Strike Finder) detect the electromagnetic signature of lightning discharges and map storm cell bearing and activity level on a cockpit display, enabling crews to identify and avoid convective weather.

multipleChoice.question: What information does an aircraft lightning detection system provide to the crew?
choices: Precise distance to storm cells in nautical miles
Wind shear intensity and recommended escape maneuvers
Turbulence severity based on G-load measurements
Bearing and relative intensity of storm cell electrical activity
multipleChoice.explanation: Lightning detectors display WHERE storms are (bearing from aircraft) and HOW ACTIVE they are (relative discharge intensity). They do not provide precise distance — just bearing and relative activity level for avoidance decision-making.

practice.question: During a night IFR flight, a pilot sees a cluster of lightning strike symbols at their 2 o'clock position on the Stormscope display. What does this indicate?
choices: Storm cell electrical activity bearing approximately 60° right of the nose
A TCAS traffic conflict at the 2 o'clock bearing
A terrain obstacle detected by the GPWS at the 2 o'clock position
A GPS waypoint 2 o'clock from the aircraft
practice.explanation: Stormscope symbols represent detected lightning discharges — their position indicates bearing from the aircraft. A cluster at 2 o'clock means convective activity approximately 60° right of the nose. The pilot should plan to deviate left or increase lateral separation from that bearing.

preassessment.question: What does a lightning detection system display?
choices: Precise storm distance
Storm bearing and relative intensity
Wind shear and turbulence
Terrain obstacles

questions.learningObjective: Compare ADF/NDB to VOR — NDB less accurate, more interference
questions.fullObjective: Compare ADF/NDB navigation to VOR, identifying NDB as generally less accurate, more susceptible to interference, and limited to non-directional bearing information without precise course deviation data.

flashcard.definition: NDB (Non-Directional Beacon) + ADF (Automatic Direction Finder): less accurate than VOR, susceptible to thunderstorm interference (lightning causes false bearings), and only provides bearing TO the station — no course deviation bar. VOR provides more accurate radial/course deviation data.
flashcard.analogy: NDB is like a campfire you navigate toward — you can tell direction but storms and other fires can confuse you. VOR is a precise lighthouse beam you can track exactly.

jeopardy.question: Compared to VOR, this navigation system is less accurate, more susceptible to electrical interference, and only provides bearing TO the station without course deviation data.
choices: GPS
ILS
DME
ADF/NDB
jeopardy.explanation: ADF/NDB navigation has two key limitations vs. VOR: (1) greater susceptibility to interference (especially thunderstorms, which cause the ADF needle to deflect toward lightning), and (2) it only gives a bearing pointer TO the station — no course deviation bar for precise tracking.

multipleChoice.question: Which statement best compares ADF/NDB navigation to VOR?
choices: NDB is less accurate, more susceptible to interference, and only provides bearing without course deviation
NDB and VOR are equally accurate but NDB has greater range
NDB is more accurate than VOR and provides both bearing and course deviation
NDB operates on VHF frequencies and is generally more reliable than VOR
multipleChoice.explanation: NDB/ADF: lower frequency (LF/MF), less accurate, highly susceptible to thunderstorm interference (ADF needle points at lightning), and only shows bearing TO the station. VOR: VHF, more accurate, provides both bearing and course deviation indication.

practice.question: A student asks why ADF/NDB approaches have higher minimums than VOR approaches. What is the most accurate explanation?
choices: NDB stations are always farther from the runway than VOR stations
NDB/ADF navigation is less accurate and more susceptible to interference than VOR, requiring higher approach minimums to maintain safety
NDB requires more avionics equipment, making installation in smaller aircraft impractical
ADF antennas are less reliable and fail more often than VOR antennas
practice.explanation: NDB approaches have higher minimums primarily because NDB/ADF navigation is inherently less accurate than VOR. The lower frequency is susceptible to atmospheric interference, and the system only provides a bearing pointer without a precise course deviation indicator, reducing lateral precision.

preassessment.question: How does ADF/NDB compare to VOR?
choices: NDB is more accurate with course deviation data
Both equally accurate, NDB has more range
NDB is less accurate, more interference-susceptible, bearing only
NDB uses VHF and is more reliable

questions.learningObjective: VOR ground test with ramp tester procedure
questions.fullObjective: Describe the ground testing procedure for a VOR navigation system using a VOR ramp tester to verify receiver accuracy on all selected radials before return to service.

flashcard.definition: Using a VOR ramp tester: place tester near antenna, set tester to desired radial, observe CDI response. Check multiple radials (at minimum 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°). Receiver accuracy per 14 CFR 91.171 must be within ±4° for VOR checkpoints.
flashcard.analogy: Like calibrating a scale with known weights — you test against known values at multiple points to confirm accuracy.

jeopardy.question: Before returning a VOR system to service, a technician uses this type of test equipment to verify receiver accuracy on multiple radials on the ground.
choices: VOR ramp tester
ILS localizer test set
ARINC 429 bus analyzer
GPS signal simulator
jeopardy.explanation: A VOR ramp tester generates a simulated VOR signal that the aircraft receiver processes. The tech selects various radials on the tester and verifies the CDI accurately reflects each selected radial before approving the system for return to service.

multipleChoice.question: What is the purpose of using a VOR ramp tester during a post-maintenance check?
choices: To transmit a VOR signal to nearby ATC for coordination
To simulate VOR station signals and verify the receiver accurately displays each tested radial
To verify GPS integrity monitoring is working correctly
To check the aircraft's ILS glideslope sensitivity on the ground
multipleChoice.explanation: A VOR ramp tester simulates VOR ground station signals so the technician can verify receiver accuracy on the ground at multiple radials without requiring the aircraft to be at a VOR checkpoint location. This is a standard post-maintenance check before RTS.

practice.question: After replacing a VOR receiver, what should the tech do before signing off the return to service?
choices: Perform only a visual inspection of the antenna and connectors
Fly the aircraft to a VOR checkpoint airborne to verify accuracy
Use a VOR ramp tester on the ground to verify receiver accuracy at multiple radials
Log the part number and call it done — no functional test is needed
practice.explanation: After any VOR receiver replacement, a functional test using a VOR ramp tester is required before RTS. The tester simulates VOR signals; the tech verifies CDI accuracy at multiple radials. This can be done on the ground and is faster and more thorough than flying to an airborne checkpoint.

preassessment.question: How is a VOR receiver tested on the ground after maintenance?
choices: Visual antenna inspection only
Fly to VOR airborne checkpoint
Log part number — no test needed
Use VOR ramp tester to simulate signals and verify accuracy

questions.learningObjective: ILS provides both vertical and horizontal approach guidance
questions.fullObjective: State that the Instrument Landing System (ILS) provides both vertical (glideslope) and horizontal (localizer) guidance during a precision instrument approach to landing.

flashcard.definition: ILS = Localizer (horizontal/lateral guidance to runway centerline) + Glideslope (vertical guidance, nominally 3° descent path). Together they guide the aircraft to the runway in low visibility. ILS is a precision approach system.
flashcard.analogy: Like railroad tracks leading into a tunnel — two rails (lateral and vertical guidance) that narrow to the exact landing point.

jeopardy.question: This precision approach system combines a localizer for horizontal guidance and a glideslope for vertical guidance during an instrument approach to landing.
choices: VOR/DME approach
Instrument Landing System (ILS)
GPS LNAV approach
NDB approach
jeopardy.explanation: ILS is a precision approach system providing two axes of guidance: the localizer (lateral — aligns aircraft with runway centerline) and glideslope (vertical — provides the standard 3° descent path to the touchdown zone).

multipleChoice.question: What two guidance signals does an ILS provide during a precision approach?
choices: Bearing and distance to the runway
Vertical and lateral wind shear alerts
Horizontal (localizer) and vertical (glideslope) guidance
Groundspeed and altitude above touchdown zone
multipleChoice.explanation: ILS provides exactly two navigation axes: localizer (horizontal, lateral — keeps you on the runway centerline extended) and glideslope (vertical — keeps you on the ~3° descent path). These together define the precise 3D flight path to the runway.

practice.question: A student tech is labeling an ILS wiring diagram. One wire goes to the CDI's left/right needle; another goes to the CDI's up/down needle. What are these two signals?
choices: DME distance for the left/right needle and radar altimeter for up/down
VOR radial for left/right and DME for up/down
RNAV approach path for both needles combined
Localizer for left/right lateral guidance and glideslope for up/down vertical guidance
practice.explanation: On an ILS CDI display: the left/right (lateral) needle is the localizer signal; the up/down (vertical) needle is the glideslope signal. These are separate receiver channels in the NAV receiver, with the localizer on the ILS frequency and glideslope automatically paired.

preassessment.question: What two guidance signals does ILS provide?
choices: Horizontal localizer and vertical glideslope
Wind shear vertical and lateral
Bearing and distance
Groundspeed and altitude

questions.learningObjective: VOR provides magnetic bearing to/from station via radials
questions.fullObjective: Explain that VOR navigation provides pilots with magnetic bearing information to and from a VOR ground station, enabling course tracking along specific radials.

flashcard.definition: A VOR radial is a magnetic bearing FROM the station (e.g., the 090° radial extends due east from the station). The OBS selects a radial; the CDI shows whether you're left or right of it. TO/FROM flag indicates your position relative to the station.
flashcard.analogy: Think of a VOR as a clock on the ground — each radial is a ray from the clock center to each minute mark around the dial.

jeopardy.question: VOR ground stations transmit signals that allow pilots to determine this type of information for course tracking along specific radials.
choices: Distance to the station in nautical miles
Pressure altitude above the station
Magnetic bearing to and from the station
Groundspeed along the selected course
jeopardy.explanation: VOR provides magnetic bearing information — specifically, radials that are magnetic bearings FROM the station. A pilot can use VOR to track a specific course to or from a station by selecting the desired radial with the OBS.

multipleChoice.question: What navigation information does a VOR ground station provide to aircraft?
choices: GPS-quality position with vertical guidance
Slant-range distance to the station
Wind speed and direction above the station
Magnetic bearing to and from the station along specific radials
multipleChoice.explanation: VOR provides magnetic bearing/course information only — it tells you what magnetic direction leads to or from the station, not distance. DME adds distance; ILS adds precision approach guidance. VOR alone gives position on a radial but not how far along the radial.

practice.question: A pilot is tracking inbound on the 270° radial of a VOR. What does this mean in terms of the aircraft's position and heading?
choices: The aircraft is west of the station, flying eastbound toward it
The aircraft is east of the station, flying westbound toward it
The aircraft is north of the station, flying southbound
The 270° radial is south of the station — the aircraft is flying north
practice.explanation: The 270° radial extends due WEST from the station (magnetic bearing of 270° FROM the station). Flying INBOUND on the 270° radial means flying EASTBOUND (090°) TOWARD the station from a position west of it. Radials are FROM the station; inbound course is the reciprocal.

preassessment.question: What does VOR navigation provide?
choices: GPS-quality position with vertical guidance
Magnetic bearing to/from station along radials
Slant-range distance
Wind speed and direction

questions.learningObjective: Optimal VHF antenna mounting location
questions.fullObjective: State that the optimal mounting location for a VHF communication antenna is low on the aircraft belly or on top of the fuselage, away from other antennas and metal structures that could cause shadowing.

flashcard.definition: Optimal locations: bottom of fuselage (belly) for ground-to-air and below-horizon communications, or top of fuselage for air-to-air. Must be away from other antennas and large metal structures to avoid shadowing (signal blockage) that reduces effective range.
flashcard.analogy: Like positioning a wireless router away from metal appliances — nearby metal blocks signals in specific directions.

jeopardy.question: For best VHF communication coverage, antennas should be mounted here, away from other antennas and metal structures that cause signal shadowing.
choices: Inside the avionics bay for protection from weather
On the wingtip to maximize separation from the fuselage
On the horizontal stabilizer for tail-away transmission
Low on the belly or on top of the fuselage, clear of obstructions
jeopardy.explanation: VHF antennas mounted on the belly provide line-of-sight to ground stations; top-mounted antennas communicate with aircraft overhead. Both locations must be away from metal structures that would shadow (block) portions of the radiation pattern.

multipleChoice.question: Where should a VHF communication antenna be mounted for optimal performance?
choices: Low on the belly or on top of the fuselage, away from other antennas and metal structures
Adjacent to the GPS antenna to share the same ground plane
Inside a radome for protection from rain and ice
On the nose of the aircraft pointing forward for maximum forward range
multipleChoice.explanation: VHF antennas need clear line-of-sight to communicate. Belly-mounted antennas serve ground communications; top-mounted serve air-to-air. Both must be separated from other antennas (interference) and metal structures (shadowing). Inside a radome or near obstructions degrades performance.

practice.question: A tech is planning the antenna layout for a new avionics installation. Where should the VHF comm antenna be placed?
choices: On the wingtip — maximum separation from nav antennas on the fuselage
Low on the belly or on top of fuselage, clear of other antennas and large metal structures
On the nose in a radome shared with the weather radar
Adjacent to the transponder antenna for combined mode S operation
practice.explanation: VHF comm antennas should be on the belly (for ATC ground comms) or top fuselage (for air-to-air), separated from other antennas by at least 6 inches minimum (preferably more) and clear of metal structures that would shadow the radiation pattern in any quadrant.

preassessment.question: Where is the optimal location for a VHF comm antenna?
choices: Inside avionics bay
Wingtip
Belly or top fuselage, clear of obstructions
Horizontal stabilizer

questions.learningObjective: Squelch mutes noise below signal threshold
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the squelch control in a VHF radio system sets the threshold below which received signal noise is muted, preventing continuous static in the headset when no signal is present.

flashcard.definition: Squelch sets the minimum signal strength required to open (unmute) the audio output. When no transmission is present, weak background noise stays below the squelch threshold and the headset is silent. A strong incoming signal opens the squelch and audio passes through.
flashcard.analogy: Like a noise gate on an audio mixer — it only lets sound through when the level exceeds a set threshold.

jeopardy.question: This VHF radio control sets the minimum signal threshold, muting background noise when no transmission is present.
choices: Squelch control
Volume control
SIDETONE adjustment
PTT sensitivity setting
jeopardy.explanation: The squelch control sets the carrier or signal threshold that must be exceeded before audio is passed to the headset. When no one is transmitting, weak RF noise stays below the threshold and the headset remains quiet — no continuous static.

multipleChoice.question: What does the squelch control on a VHF radio do?
choices: Sets the audio volume for headset output
Sets the threshold below which received noise is muted when no signal is present
Adjusts the transmitter power output level
Controls the SIDETONE level for microphone monitoring
multipleChoice.explanation: Squelch mutes the receiver audio until a signal exceeds the set threshold. Too little squelch: constant static. Too much squelch: weak but valid signals are cut off. Proper squelch setting provides a quiet background with clean response to incoming transmissions.

practice.question: A pilot complains of constant static in the headset even when no one is transmitting. What should the technician adjust?
choices: Increase the radio volume control
Replace the COM antenna — static indicates an open antenna
Increase the squelch threshold so background noise is muted
Adjust the SIDETONE level to reduce self-noise
practice.explanation: Constant static with no incoming transmission means the squelch threshold is set too low — background RF noise is opening the audio gate. Increasing the squelch sets a higher threshold that noise can't cross, but valid transmissions still can. Verify the squelch isn't set so high it cuts off weak but valid ATC transmissions.

preassessment.question: What does squelch control do?
choices: Sets audio volume
Adjusts transmitter power
Controls SIDETONE level
Mutes noise when no signal present

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose intermittent/noisy comm audio — connector, coax, mic jack
questions.fullObjective: Given intermittent or noisy audio in a communication system, determine that the most common causes are a loose or corroded connector, damaged coaxial cable, or failing microphone jack.

flashcard.definition: Three most common causes of intermittent or noisy comm audio: (1) loose or corroded connector (high contact resistance causing noise), (2) damaged coaxial cable (bent, kinked, or crushed center conductor), (3) failing microphone jack (worn or oxidized contacts causing noise when moving).
flashcard.analogy: Like a lamp that flickers — most often it's a loose bulb, damaged cord, or worn socket, not the lamp itself.

jeopardy.question: Loose corroded connectors, damaged coaxial cable, and failing microphone jacks are the most common causes of this aircraft comm system symptom.
choices: Stuck microphone transmitting continuously
Intermittent or noisy received audio
Total loss of both transmit and receive capability
Radio tuning to incorrect frequency
jeopardy.explanation: Intermittent or noisy audio points to physical connection problems. Corroded connectors and damaged coax create resistance that introduces noise. A failing mic jack causes noise that varies with microphone movement — all three are physical/mechanical rather than electronic failures.

multipleChoice.question: What are the most common causes of intermittent or noisy audio in an aircraft communication system?
choices: Incorrectly programmed squelch threshold and volume setting
Software version mismatch between the radio and audio panel
Loose or corroded connectors, damaged coaxial cable, or failing microphone jack
Alternator noise from an unfiltered electrical bus
multipleChoice.explanation: The vast majority of intermittent/noisy comm audio issues are physical connection problems: corroded or loose connectors create variable resistance; damaged coax (kinked, crushed, water-infiltrated) degrades signal; failing mic jacks introduce noise with movement. These should be the first inspection points.

practice.question: A pilot reports that comm audio crackles when the microphone is moved or touched. What should the tech inspect first?
choices: The SIDETONE circuit board inside the COM radio
The squelch threshold calibration on the audio panel
The antenna coaxial cable on the fuselage exterior
The microphone jack and connector — movement-sensitive noise indicates a failing jack contact
practice.explanation: Noise that correlates with physical movement of the microphone is the classic symptom of a failing microphone jack. Worn or oxidized contacts in the jack make intermittent contact — movement interrupts or varies the connection. Inspect and clean or replace the jack before investigating the radio itself.

preassessment.question: Most common causes of intermittent comm audio are:
choices: Loose connectors, damaged coax, or failing mic jack
Software version mismatch
Squelch and volume settings
Unfiltered alternator noise

questions.learningObjective: VHF antenna inspection checklist
questions.fullObjective: Describe what to check during regular communication antenna inspections: physical condition, secure mounting, connector tightness, coaxial cable condition, and absence of corrosion.

flashcard.definition: Regular VHF antenna inspection: (1) physical condition — no cracks, chips, or broken base; (2) secure mounting — no looseness; (3) connector tightness — coax connector properly torqued; (4) coaxial cable condition — no kinks, chafing, or water infiltration; (5) no corrosion at base or connector.
flashcard.analogy: Like inspecting a car antenna — check the element, the base mounting, and the cable all the way to where it connects to the radio.

jeopardy.question: Physical condition, secure mounting, connector tightness, coaxial cable integrity, and absence of corrosion are all checked during this routine task.
choices: ILS glideslope alignment check
Transponder ground test
VHF antenna preflight inspection
GPS RAIM prediction check
jeopardy.explanation: VHF antenna inspections cover all five items: physical element condition (no cracks), mounting security (no play), coax connector torque, cable inspection for damage, and corrosion check at the base and connector — all of which affect antenna performance and longevity.

multipleChoice.question: What should be checked during a regular VHF communication antenna inspection?
choices: Antenna impedance using a network analyzer and SWR meter
The antenna radiation pattern using a calibrated antenna range
The radio's transmitted power output and frequency accuracy
Physical condition, mounting security, connector tightness, coax condition, and corrosion
multipleChoice.explanation: A standard VHF antenna inspection is visual and physical: check for physical damage to the element, verify it's securely mounted with no wobble, confirm the coax connector is tight, inspect the coax for damage, and check for corrosion at all contact points. An SWR measurement can be added after any maintenance.

practice.question: During a routine avionics inspection, a tech notices the VHF comm antenna has some paint flaking at the base and the coax connector feels slightly loose. What actions are required?
choices: Clean and inspect the base for corrosion, torque the connector to specification, and recheck physical integrity
Replace the entire antenna immediately
Document the finding and defer — these are cosmetic issues only
Only re-torque the connector — flaking paint near antennas is always cosmetic
practice.explanation: Flaking paint at the antenna base can indicate corrosion starting underneath. The connector must be torqued per spec to ensure proper RF contact and waterproofing. Both conditions should be addressed and documented — deferred items that progress can cause significant degradation in antenna performance.

preassessment.question: What is checked during a VHF antenna inspection?
choices: SWR and radiated power
Physical condition, mounting, connector, coax, and corrosion
Transmit power and frequency
Radiation pattern range test

questions.learningObjective: RF radiation safety — system off during antenna work
questions.fullObjective: State that RF radiation is a significant safety concern when working on aircraft systems that emit radio frequency signals, and that the system must be off during antenna work.

flashcard.definition: Radio frequency (RF) radiation from transmitting antennas can cause tissue heating, eye damage, and interference with medical devices at close range. ALWAYS de-energize the transmitter before working on or near the antenna. This applies to all RF systems: VHF comm, transponder, DME, ADS-B.
flashcard.analogy: Like not looking directly into a laser — the energy is invisible but can cause real harm at close range.

jeopardy.question: When working on or near a VHF antenna or any RF-transmitting system on an aircraft, this safety measure is required.
choices: Wear rubber gloves only — RF is an electrical hazard
Ground the antenna with a static wick
Keep transmitter in standby mode at reduced power
De-energize (turn off) the transmitting system
jeopardy.explanation: RF radiation from aircraft transmitters can cause thermal injury to body tissues at close range (especially eyes). The system must be completely OFF — not in standby — during any work on or near the antenna or transmission line.

multipleChoice.question: What is the key safety requirement when performing maintenance near an aircraft VHF antenna?
choices: Ensure the RF transmitting system is off before working near the antenna
Keep the system in low-power standby mode to reduce risk
Wear RF-shielded gloves rated for VHF frequencies
Only approach the antenna from behind to avoid the main radiation lobe
multipleChoice.explanation: The only safe approach is to de-energize the system. RF can cause tissue injury at close range — 'standby' or 'low power' modes still transmit and are not safe for nearby work. Always verify the system is off before working at the antenna or along the transmission line.

practice.question: An avionics tech is about to replace a VHF blade antenna on top of the fuselage. What must be done before touching the antenna?
choices: Set the COM radio to the guard frequency (121.5 MHz) as a safety check
Ensure the aircraft master switch and all COM radios are off
Connect a static ground strap to the fuselage
Wear a VHF-rated RF shield vest
practice.explanation: Before touching any transmitting antenna or its feedline, the system must be completely off. A COM radio transmitting at close range can cause tissue heating — particularly eye injury. Master switch off and all radios verified off (or circuit breakers pulled) is the correct precaution.

preassessment.question: What must be done before working near an RF transmitting antenna?
choices: Wear RF-rated gloves
Set to low-power standby
De-energize (turn off) the transmitting system
Approach from behind only

questions.learningObjective: VHF comm frequency range 118.0–136.975 MHz
questions.fullObjective: State that VHF communication in general aviation uses the frequency range of 118.0 MHz to 136.975 MHz.

flashcard.definition: Aviation VHF communication: 118.0 MHz to 136.975 MHz. 25 kHz channel spacing in the US; 8.33 kHz in Europe/high-density airspace. AM modulation. Guard frequency: 121.5 MHz (emergency). Military guard: 243.0 MHz (UHF).
flashcard.analogy: Like a specific band on a radio dial reserved exclusively for aviation — 118 to 137 MHz belongs to air traffic.

jeopardy.question: General aviation VHF communications use this frequency range, beginning at 118.0 MHz.
jeopardy.explanation: Aviation VHF comm is 118.0–136.975 MHz. The range 108.0–117.95 MHz is shared by VOR and ILS (nav frequencies). 329–335 MHz is glideslope. 960–1215 MHz is DME and transponder.

multipleChoice.question: What is the frequency range for VHF communication in general aviation?
choices: 329 to 335 MHz
960 to 1215 MHz
multipleChoice.explanation: VHF communication in aviation occupies 118.0–136.975 MHz. This is adjacent to but distinct from the navigation band (108–118 MHz). All aviation voice communications with ATC, ATIS, UNICOM, and other aircraft use this frequency range.

practice.question: A student asks why their new COM radio shows frequencies from 118.000 to 136.975 but their NAV radio shows 108.00 to 117.95. Why are they different?
choices: They are the same band — one shows MHz and one shows kHz
The NAV radio is tuned to AM broadcast while the COM radio is aviation only
COM radios cover the aviation communication band (118–136.975 MHz) while NAV radios cover the navigation band (108–117.95 MHz) for VOR and ILS
COM operates in the UHF range and NAV in the VHF range
practice.explanation: Aviation uses two adjacent VHF bands: 108–117.95 MHz for navigation (VOR and ILS localizer) and 118–136.975 MHz for communication (ATC, UNICOM, ATIS). Modern avionics combine both in a single unit but they remain separate frequency bands with different purposes.

preassessment.question: What is the VHF comm frequency range in aviation?

questions.learningObjective: Coaxial cable replacement — impedance, connectors, SWR
questions.fullObjective: State the critical steps when replacing coaxial cable for a communication antenna: use the correct impedance and cable type, properly install and torque connectors, and verify SWR after installation.

flashcard.definition: When replacing coax for a VHF antenna: (1) use correct impedance cable (50Ω for VHF comm), (2) use correct cable type/spec for the run length and environment, (3) install connectors properly — correct preparation, crimping, and torque, (4) measure SWR (Standing Wave Ratio) to verify the installation.
flashcard.analogy: Like replacing a garden hose — use the right diameter (impedance), connect fittings properly (connectors), then check for leaks (SWR).

jeopardy.question: When replacing coaxial cable for a VHF antenna, correct impedance, proper connector installation and torque, and verifying this measurement are all required.
choices: Antenna radiation pattern
VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio)
Signal-to-noise ratio at the receiver
Transmitter power output
jeopardy.explanation: After coax replacement, SWR (Standing Wave Ratio, also called VSWR) must be measured to verify the antenna-cable system is properly matched. An SWR above 1.5:1 indicates a mismatch — caused by wrong impedance cable, damaged connector, or antenna fault.

multipleChoice.question: What are the critical steps when replacing coaxial cable on a VHF communication antenna?
choices: Only match cable length to the original; impedance doesn't matter for short runs
Replace the antenna at the same time for guaranteed performance
Use correct impedance and cable type, install and torque connectors properly, verify SWR after installation
Only SWR verification is required — cable type and connector torque are secondary
multipleChoice.explanation: All three steps are critical: wrong impedance creates a mismatch (increased reflected power, reduced range); improperly installed connectors cause intermittent connection and RF leakage; without SWR verification, a hidden fault may not be detected until the radio fails in flight.

practice.question: After replacing the coax run to a VHF comm antenna, a tech measures SWR of 3.2:1. What does this indicate and what should be done?
choices: SWR of 3.2:1 is acceptable for VHF — no action needed
Re-torque the coax connector at the antenna and the reading will come down to acceptable levels
SWR only matters for high-power HF transmitters — VHF SWR readings above 2:1 are normal
High SWR indicates a severe impedance mismatch — inspect connectors, verify cable impedance, and recheck the antenna
practice.explanation: SWR of 3.2:1 indicates a significant mismatch — ideally SWR should be below 1.5:1 for VHF systems. High SWR means substantial reflected power, reducing transmitted power and range. Inspect: connector installation and condition, verify 50Ω cable was used, check antenna for damage.

preassessment.question: Critical steps for coax replacement on a VHF antenna include:
choices: Correct impedance, proper connectors, verify SWR
Match cable length only — impedance doesn't matter
Replace antenna simultaneously
SWR only — connector torque is secondary

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose stuck mic — constant tone blocks reception
questions.fullObjective: Given a constant tone heard instead of an ATC response, diagnose a stuck microphone keeping the transmitter keyed and blocking radio reception as the most likely cause.

flashcard.definition: A stuck mic occurs when the PTT switch fails in the closed (keyed) position, keeping the transmitter continuously active. This blocks ALL reception on the frequency — you cannot hear ATC. Also blocks other aircraft from transmitting (the whole frequency is 'full'). Symptom: continuous sidetone, no ATC reception.
flashcard.analogy: Like holding down a talk button on a walkie-talkie — no one else can get through, and you can't hear anything back.

jeopardy.question: When a pilot hears a constant tone but cannot hear ATC responses, this failure — keeping the transmitter continuously keyed — is the most likely diagnosis.
choices: Failed receiver squelch circuit
Frequency selector stuck between channels
Stuck microphone PTT keeping the transmitter keyed
Audio panel failure routing TX audio to headset
jeopardy.explanation: A stuck PTT keeps the transmitter on continuously, which blocks the receiver on the same frequency (you can't transmit and receive simultaneously on one radio). The constant sidetone in the headset is the transmitter sending back the mic signal. Other aircraft on the frequency also cannot transmit.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot reports hearing a constant tone in the headset and is unable to receive any ATC transmissions. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The squelch is set too high, blocking all incoming signals
The volume control is set too low to hear ATC
The receiver has failed — the constant tone is from the squelch circuit hunting
A stuck microphone PTT is keeping the transmitter keyed, blocking reception
multipleChoice.explanation: Constant sidetone with no received audio is the signature of a stuck PTT/mic. The transmitter is locked on — you hear your own side tone continuously, and the receiver cannot receive because the radio is in transmit mode. Other aircraft on the frequency will also report the frequency being 'stepped on' with a continuous tone.

practice.question: During flight, a pilot loses contact with ATC. The headset has a constant tone and all transmit attempts sound normal on sidetone. Other aircraft on the frequency comment 'someone is stepping on the frequency.' What should the tech inspect after the flight?
choices: The microphone PTT switch and its wiring — stuck in the keyed position
The squelch potentiometer — it has failed to the fully open position
The VHF receiver — it has failed and is injecting a constant noise tone
The audio panel — it is misrouting transmit audio back to the headset input
practice.explanation: All three clues — constant tone for the pilot, inability to receive, and other aircraft reporting a continuous stepped-on frequency — confirm a stuck mic/PTT. The PTT switch or its wiring is failed in the closed (keyed) position. Inspect the PTT switch, microphone connector, and wiring between PTT and radio for a fault.

preassessment.question: A constant tone and no ATC reception most likely indicates:
choices: High squelch setting
Stuck microphone PTT keying the transmitter
Low volume setting
Failed receiver squelch hunting

questions.learningObjective: HF used for long-range oceanic communications
questions.fullObjective: Explain that HF communication is used in aviation primarily for long-range communications over oceanic and remote areas beyond VHF line-of-sight range.

flashcard.definition: HF (High Frequency, 2–30 MHz) uses ionospheric skip propagation to achieve ranges of thousands of miles — beyond VHF line-of-sight. Used primarily for oceanic and remote area communications where VHF has no coverage. SELCAL is often used with HF for crew alerting.
flashcard.analogy: VHF is like a flashlight — works great nearby but stops at the horizon. HF is like bouncing light off the ceiling — it goes much farther by reflecting off the ionosphere.

jeopardy.question: This radio frequency band is used for long-range aviation communications over oceans and remote areas where VHF line-of-sight coverage doesn't reach.
choices: ELT (406 MHz)
HF (2–30 MHz)
jeopardy.explanation: HF uses ionospheric skip to propagate signals thousands of miles — far beyond VHF's line-of-sight range limit. Oceanic tracks, polar routes, and remote continental operations use HF (or satellite communications) because ATC VHF coverage doesn't exist over open oceans.

multipleChoice.question: Why is HF communication used in aviation for oceanic flights?
choices: HF uses ionospheric skip propagation to achieve ranges of thousands of miles beyond VHF coverage
HF antennas are lighter than VHF antennas, reducing fuel burn
HF has higher audio quality than VHF for long-haul flights
HF is encrypted by default, providing security for international routes
multipleChoice.explanation: HF propagates via ionospheric skip — signals bounce off the ionosphere and return to earth thousands of miles away. This allows communication far beyond the line-of-sight horizon that limits VHF. Over the Pacific or Atlantic, VHF ground stations simply don't exist — HF or SATCOM fill the gap.

practice.question: A passenger asks why the flight deck has both VHF and HF radios. How would you explain the difference?
choices: VHF is for pilot-to-pilot comms; HF is for pilot-to-ATC comms
VHF works for nearby ATC communications; HF uses ionospheric bounce for ranges of thousands of miles, essential when flying over oceans out of VHF range
HF is a backup for when VHF frequencies are congested
VHF is digital and HF is analog — long-haul flights use HF for reliability
practice.explanation: VHF (line-of-sight) covers typical domestic ATC. Once over the ocean, VHF ground coverage ends. HF's ionospheric skip enables contact with Oceanic Centers thousands of miles away. Modern aircraft also use SATCOM as an alternative, but HF remains a required backup on most oceanic routes.

preassessment.question: Why is HF used on oceanic flights?
choices: Better audio quality
Lighter antennas
Ionospheric skip enables thousands-of-miles range beyond VHF coverage
Default encryption for security

questions.learningObjective: Squawk code identifies aircraft to ATC radar
questions.fullObjective: Explain that a transponder squawk code is set by the pilot to identify the aircraft to ATC radar and allow controllers to distinguish it from other traffic.

flashcard.definition: A squawk code is a 4-digit octal number (0000–7777, excluding some) assigned by ATC to identify a specific aircraft on radar. The transponder replies to radar interrogations with the code, allowing controllers to correlate the radar return with the specific flight.
flashcard.analogy: Like a name badge at a conference — the squawk code tells ATC which radar blip belongs to which aircraft.

jeopardy.question: This 4-digit code set by the pilot allows ATC radar to identify a specific aircraft and distinguish it from other traffic on the display.
choices: Transponder squawk code
ADS-B ICAO address
IFF challenge code
ELT registration number
jeopardy.explanation: The squawk code (Mode A) is a 4-digit octal identifier set on the transponder at ATC request. When the radar interrogates, the transponder replies with this code, allowing controllers to label and track the specific aircraft's radar return among many.

multipleChoice.question: What is the purpose of a transponder squawk code?
choices: To encode the aircraft's altitude in binary for TCAS processing
To allow ATC radar to identify and track a specific aircraft among other traffic
To broadcast GPS position data to nearby aircraft via ADS-B
To encrypt the transponder reply to prevent spoofing
multipleChoice.explanation: The squawk code is an identification tool — it lets ATC correlate a specific radar return with a specific flight. Without squawk, the radar shows only anonymous returns; with squawk, ATC can say 'that's N12345' and track it with callsign, altitude, and flight data attached.

practice.question: ATC tells a pilot to 'squawk 4521.' What does the pilot do and what does ATC see after?
choices: Enter 4521 in the radio as a communication frequency
Broadcast the code 4521 on 121.5 MHz to alert nearby traffic
Set transponder to code 4521; ATC radar will now label that aircraft's return with its flight ID and show the assigned code
Set the GPS direct-to waypoint with identifier 4521
practice.explanation: The pilot sets the transponder code to 4521. ATC's secondary surveillance radar interrogates all transponders; N12345's unit replies with 4521. The radar computer correlates the reply to the flight plan, labels the radar return, and the controller can now positively identify and track that aircraft.

preassessment.question: What is a transponder squawk code used for?
choices: Encode altitude for TCAS
Encrypt transponder replies
Broadcast GPS position via ADS-B
Identify specific aircraft on ATC radar

questions.learningObjective: TCAS monitors traffic and provides collision avoidance RAs
questions.fullObjective: State that TCAS (Traffic Collision Avoidance System) monitors nearby transponder-equipped aircraft and provides resolution advisories to prevent mid-air collisions.

flashcard.definition: TCAS interrogates nearby aircraft transponders and tracks their position. Issues: Traffic Advisories (TA) for awareness, and Resolution Advisories (RA) with specific vertical maneuver instructions to prevent mid-air collision. TCAS operates independently of ATC.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car's collision avoidance system that warns you and then says 'brake NOW' — it doesn't wait for a traffic light.

jeopardy.question: This airborne collision avoidance system monitors nearby transponder-equipped aircraft and issues resolution advisories to prevent mid-air collisions.
choices: ADS-B In traffic display
TCAS (Traffic Collision Avoidance System)
GPWS terrain warning system
ATC radar separation service
jeopardy.explanation: TCAS actively interrogates nearby transponders, computes collision risk, and issues TAs (awareness) and RAs (climb/descend instructions) to the crew. TCAS RA instructions override ATC instructions — crews must follow TCAS RAs immediately.

multipleChoice.question: What does TCAS do?
choices: Displays ATC-assigned squawk codes from other aircraft on the ND
Transmits ADS-B position data to nearby aircraft
Monitors nearby transponder-equipped aircraft and provides RAs to prevent collision
Alerts crew when another aircraft is broadcasting an emergency squawk
multipleChoice.explanation: TCAS interrogates, tracks, computes threat, and advises. TAs warn of traffic; RAs instruct a specific vertical maneuver (climb or descend). TCAS coordinates with the intruder's TCAS (if equipped) to ensure complementary maneuvers — one climbs while the other descends.

practice.question: TCAS issues a 'CLIMB CLIMB' RA while ATC has just issued a descent instruction. What should the crew do?
choices: Follow ATC — ATC has overall airspace responsibility and supersedes TCAS
Ignore both and maintain altitude until the conflict is clearer
Request clarification from ATC before initiating any maneuver
Follow the TCAS RA — climb immediately, then notify ATC
practice.explanation: TCAS RAs take precedence over ATC instructions. The crew must execute the RA immediately — delaying or following a conflicting ATC instruction could result in a collision. After completing the RA maneuver, notify ATC: 'TCAS RA, returning to assigned altitude.'

preassessment.question: What does TCAS provide?
choices: Collision avoidance resolution advisories
ADS-B position broadcasts
ATC squawk code display
Emergency squawk alerts

questions.learningObjective: Primary transponder purpose — reply to ATC SSR interrogations
questions.fullObjective: State that the primary purpose of an aircraft transponder is to reply to ATC secondary surveillance radar interrogations, allowing controllers to positively identify and track the aircraft.

flashcard.definition: The transponder replies to Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) interrogations from ATC ground stations. SSR interrogates at 1030 MHz; transponder replies at 1090 MHz with squawk code and altitude. This allows ATC to positively identify and track the aircraft on radar.
flashcard.analogy: Like a ship's IFF system — when the radar pings you, you reply with your identity code so the controller knows who you are.

jeopardy.question: Replying to ATC secondary surveillance radar interrogations to allow positive aircraft identification and tracking is the primary purpose of this cockpit device.
choices: ELT (Emergency Locator Transmitter)
ADS-B Out unit
Transponder
DME interrogator
jeopardy.explanation: The transponder responds to ATC SSR interrogations at 1030 MHz with replies at 1090 MHz containing squawk code and altitude. This active response allows ATC to positively identify each aircraft rather than relying on passive primary radar returns alone.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of an aircraft transponder?
choices: To broadcast GPS position data to other aircraft
To encrypt aircraft identification data for security purposes
To monitor other transponder-equipped aircraft for collision avoidance
To reply to ATC secondary surveillance radar interrogations for identification and tracking
multipleChoice.explanation: The transponder is fundamentally a reply device — it listens for SSR interrogations and responds with coded information (squawk code in Mode A, altitude in Mode C, data link in Mode S). This allows ATC secondary radar to create the labeled radar display controllers use for separation.

practice.question: Why do aircraft need transponders in addition to the ATC primary radar that can detect them without transponders?
choices: Transponder replies allow ATC to identify specific aircraft and get altitude data, which primary radar echoes alone don't provide
Primary radar is illegal for civilian ATC use — transponders are the only legal method
Primary radar only works during daylight — transponders provide 24-hour coverage
Transponders have longer range than primary radar, covering more airspace
practice.explanation: Primary radar shows anonymous returns — blips with no identity or altitude. Transponders add identity (squawk code) and altitude (Mode C), creating a labeled, 3D-position-aware picture. Mode S adds data link for more detailed information. Both systems are used together for complete ATC surveillance.

preassessment.question: What is the primary purpose of a transponder?
choices: Broadcast GPS position
Reply to ATC SSR for identification and tracking
Monitor other aircraft for collision avoidance
Encrypt aircraft identification

questions.learningObjective: Transponder antenna installation — impedance, coax, torque
questions.fullObjective: Apply the correct installation step when installing a new digital transponder antenna: verify antenna impedance match, use the correct coaxial cable type, and torque connectors to specification.

flashcard.definition: Transponder antennas operate at 1030/1090 MHz. Installation: (1) verify 50Ω impedance match, (2) use appropriate coax (low-loss type at ~1 GHz, e.g., RG-400 or similar), (3) torque TNC or BNC connectors per spec, (4) verify SWR after installation.
flashcard.analogy: Like installing a high-frequency speaker — you need the right impedance cable and proper connections or you'll lose signal before it gets there.

jeopardy.question: When installing a transponder antenna, verifying impedance match, using the correct coaxial cable, and torquing connectors to spec are all part of this procedure.
choices: VOR ramp test pre-check
ADS-B link budget calculation
ILS glideslope antenna alignment
Transponder antenna installation
jeopardy.explanation: Transponder antenna installation at 1030/1090 MHz requires: 50Ω impedance match (antenna and cable), correct low-loss coax for ~1 GHz operation, and properly installed/torqued connectors to ensure RF integrity and prevent water ingress.

multipleChoice.question: What must be verified when installing a transponder antenna?
choices: Antenna impedance match, correct coaxial cable type, and connector torque to specification
Only that the physical mounting is secure — RF performance self-adjusts
That the antenna is co-located with the VOR antenna for shared ground plane
Only the coax length — connector type doesn't matter at transponder frequencies
multipleChoice.explanation: All three are required: impedance match ensures proper power transfer and minimal reflection; correct coax minimizes loss at ~1 GHz (standard RG-58 has too much loss); proper connector torque ensures electrical contact and weatherproofing. Ignoring any of these risks poor transponder performance or compliance failure.

practice.question: After installing a new transponder antenna with a length of RG-58 coax and new TNC connectors, the ramp test shows weak reply strength. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The transponder transmit power needs recalibration
RG-58 has high loss at transponder frequencies (~1 GHz) — use low-loss coax like RG-400
The transponder antenna is mounted too close to the fuselage
The TNC connectors are the wrong type — replace with BNC connectors
practice.explanation: RG-58 has significant loss at 1 GHz frequencies. For transponder/DME/ADS-B antennas (1030/1090 MHz), low-loss cable (RG-400, RG-142, or equivalent) should be used. The longer the run, the more critical this becomes. High cable loss = weak replies = potential transponder failure on ATC equipment.

preassessment.question: Transponder antenna installation requires:
choices: Secure mounting only — RF self-adjusts
Co-location with VOR antenna
Impedance match, correct coax, and connector torque
Matching coax length only

questions.learningObjective: Mode S 24-bit address enables selective interrogation
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the 24-bit address in a Mode S transponder is a unique identifier assigned to a specific aircraft, allowing ATC systems to selectively interrogate and track individual aircraft.

flashcard.definition: Mode S assigns each aircraft a unique 24-bit ICAO address (set during manufacture/registration). ATC systems use this to selectively interrogate specific aircraft (not all-call interrogation like Mode A/C). Enables data link, reduces RF congestion, and supports ADS-B Out transmission.
flashcard.analogy: Like a cell phone number — it's yours alone, so the tower can call just you rather than broadcasting to everyone.

jeopardy.question: This unique 24-bit identifier assigned to a Mode S transponder allows ATC systems to selectively interrogate a specific aircraft rather than broadcast to all transponders.
choices: ICAO 24-bit address
SSID (squawk signal ID)
ADS-B Out position report
Flight ID callsign
jeopardy.explanation: The 24-bit ICAO address is unique to each aircraft globally — assigned by the country of registration. Mode S ground stations use it to address specific aircraft individually (selective interrogation), reducing frequency congestion compared to Mode A/C all-call interrogations.

multipleChoice.question: What does the 24-bit ICAO address in a Mode S transponder enable?
choices: Encoding of aircraft registration number in audio Morse code
Selective interrogation of specific aircraft by ATC systems
Automatic frequency selection for the most congested sector
Encryption of altitude data to prevent spoofing
multipleChoice.explanation: The 24-bit address enables selective interrogation — ATC can query a specific aircraft's transponder rather than broadcasting to all. This reduces RF congestion in busy airspace and enables two-way data link communication (used for ACAS coordination, ATIS data, and future applications).

practice.question: Two aircraft fly the same oceanic route, both with Mode C transponders. ATC needs to monitor one specifically. How does Mode S improve this situation?
choices: Mode S automatically assigns a unique callsign to each aircraft for voice identification
Mode S increases transponder range by using a higher frequency band
Mode S gives each aircraft a unique 24-bit ICAO address, allowing ATC to selectively interrogate and track that specific aircraft
Mode S encryption prevents other aircraft from reading ATC interrogations
practice.explanation: Mode C provides altitude but no selective address — ATC must filter by squawk code and position. Mode S's 24-bit ICAO address allows the ground system to interrogate only that specific aircraft, get data link responses, and track it individually without RF congestion from interrogating all transponders simultaneously.

preassessment.question: What does the Mode S 24-bit ICAO address allow?
choices: Morse code aircraft registration
Encrypted altitude data
Automatic frequency selection
Selective interrogation of specific aircraft

questions.learningObjective: ADS-B Out broadcasts position, altitude, velocity, ID
questions.fullObjective: List the information broadcast by ADS-B Out: the aircraft's GPS position, altitude, velocity, identification, and other state data to ATC ground stations and other ADS-B equipped aircraft.

flashcard.definition: ADS-B Out broadcasts (1090ES or UAT): GPS position, GPS altitude, pressure altitude, groundspeed, track, identification (ICAO address + flight ID), and other state data. Transmitted automatically and continuously without interrogation. Required in most controlled airspace per 14 CFR 91.225.
flashcard.analogy: Like a ship's AIS transponder — continuously broadcasting who you are, where you are, and how fast you're going to anyone listening.

jeopardy.question: GPS position, altitude, velocity, identification, and state data are all included in the continuous broadcast of this avionics system.
choices: TCAS surveillance interrogation
ADS-B Out broadcast
Mode C altitude reply
VHF comm position report
jeopardy.explanation: ADS-B Out broadcasts all of this state data automatically at approximately 1 Hz on 1090 MHz (Mode S Extended Squitter) or 978 MHz (UAT). It doesn't wait to be interrogated — it broadcasts continuously to both ATC ground stations and other ADS-B In equipped aircraft.

multipleChoice.question: What information does ADS-B Out broadcast?
choices: Only squawk code and Mode C altitude identical to a standard transponder
Encrypted flight plan data to ATC ground stations only
GPS position, altitude, velocity, and aircraft identification, plus other state data
TCAS coordination data to nearby aircraft only
multipleChoice.explanation: ADS-B Out is far richer than Mode A/C: it broadcasts GPS-derived position (latitude/longitude), GPS and pressure altitude, groundspeed, track, ICAO address, flight ID, and other state parameters — all unsolicited and continuously. This gives ATC and other aircraft a GPS-precision picture of every equipped aircraft.
