practice.question: An ATC controller wants to verify that an aircraft's ADS-B Out is working correctly. What data should they expect to see if the system is functioning?
choices: Only squawk code 1200 — ADS-B uses the VFR squawk as its transmission ID
Traffic advisories from the aircraft's TCAS system relayed through ADS-B
Only Mode C altitude — ADS-B Out supplements but doesn't include position data
GPS position, altitude, velocity, identification, and state data updating approximately once per second
practice.explanation: A properly functioning ADS-B Out system provides a complete traffic picture updating ~1 Hz: GPS position (accurate to meters), altitude, groundspeed, track angle, ICAO address, and flight identifier. ATC can confirm functionality by seeing all these data fields populated and updating on their surveillance display.

preassessment.question: What does ADS-B Out broadcast?
choices: GPS position, altitude, velocity, and ID
Squawk code only
Encrypted flight plan only
TCAS coordination data only

questions.learningObjective: Mode C transmits squawk plus pressure altitude
questions.fullObjective: Explain that Mode C transponders automatically transmit pressure altitude encoded from an encoding altimeter in addition to the Mode A identification code.

flashcard.definition: Mode C replies include Mode A squawk code PLUS pressure altitude (Gillham code from encoding altimeter or ADC). Altitude is transmitted in 100-ft increments referenced to 29.92 inHg (standard pressure). ATC can see both aircraft identity and altitude on their display.
flashcard.analogy: Mode A is like sending a text with just your name. Mode C is like sending your name AND your GPS altitude at the same time.

jeopardy.question: In addition to the Mode A squawk code, this transponder mode automatically transmits pressure altitude encoded by an encoding altimeter.
choices: Mode 1 (military)
Mode 3 (IFF)
Mode C
Mode S Extended Squitter
jeopardy.explanation: Mode C adds automatic altitude reporting to the Mode A squawk code reply. The encoding altimeter (or ADC) provides pressure altitude in Gillham code; the transponder includes this in its reply. ATC sees both identity and altitude, enabling 3D tracking.

multipleChoice.question: What does a Mode C transponder transmit in addition to the squawk code?
choices: GPS-derived latitude and longitude position
Aircraft registration number in ICAO format
Groundspeed computed from GPS data
Pressure altitude encoded from an encoding altimeter
multipleChoice.explanation: Mode C = Mode A (squawk code) + pressure altitude. The altitude comes from an encoding altimeter (mechanical Gillham code output) or air data computer, transmitted in 100-ft increments at standard pressure (29.92). ATC sees altitude tagged to each radar return.

practice.question: ATC calls a pilot and reports 'N12345, altitude readout invalid, check your altitude encoding.' What component should the technician inspect?
choices: The encoding altimeter or air data computer that provides Gillham code to the transponder
The GPS receiver — Mode C altitude comes from GPS in modern aircraft
The Mode S transponder unit itself — it has an internal altitude encoding circuit
The coaxial cable between the transponder and its antenna
practice.explanation: Mode C altitude data comes from an external source — the encoding altimeter (which generates Gillham code from aneroid capsule expansion) or ADC. The transponder itself just packages and transmits whatever altitude data it receives. An invalid altitude readout usually points to the encoder or its connection to the transponder.

preassessment.question: What does Mode C add to the Mode A squawk code reply?
choices: GPS position
Pressure altitude from encoding altimeter
Groundspeed from GPS
ICAO registration number

questions.learningObjective: Transponder antenna type and frequency range
questions.fullObjective: State that transponder antennas are typically low-profile blade or stub antennas designed to operate in the 1030/1090 MHz frequency range.

flashcard.definition: Transponder antennas are low-profile blade or stub antennas tuned to 1030/1090 MHz (L-band). Mounted on the belly (bottom of fuselage) and/or top of fuselage. Low profile to minimize drag. 50Ω impedance.
flashcard.analogy: Like a small shark fin on an aircraft belly — compact, low-drag, tuned to work at the specific L-band frequencies.

jeopardy.question: Transponder antennas are typically low-profile blade or stub antennas tuned to operate at these two frequencies.
choices: 108 and 117.95 MHz
329 and 335 MHz
1030 and 1090 MHz
jeopardy.explanation: ATC interrogates at 1030 MHz; transponders reply at 1090 MHz. The antenna must cover both frequencies. Low-profile blades or stubs are used to minimize aerodynamic drag while maintaining adequate ground plane for the omnidirectional radiation pattern needed.

multipleChoice.question: What type of antenna does a transponder use and at what frequencies?
choices: Low-profile blade or stub antenna at 1030/1090 MHz
Loop antenna at 190–535 kHz
V-shaped bent dipole at 108–118 MHz
Patch antenna at 1575 MHz
multipleChoice.explanation: Transponders operate in the L-band at 1030 MHz (interrogation) and 1090 MHz (reply). Low-profile blade or stub antennas are standard — they provide the omni pattern needed for ATC line-of-sight while minimizing drag. V-shaped dipoles are VOR; loops are ADF; patch antennas are GPS.

practice.question: During a walkaround, a tech notices a small blade antenna on the belly of the aircraft near the aft fuselage. What system does this most likely serve?
choices: ADF/NDB — ADF loop antennas are blade-shaped
Transponder — low-profile blade antennas on the belly are typical transponder installations
VOR — VOR antennas are mounted on the belly near aft fuselage
HF comm — HF antennas are low-profile blades on the belly
practice.explanation: Low-profile blade antennas on the belly near the aft fuselage are the standard transponder antenna location. VOR antennas are V-shaped dipoles on the fin or fuselage. ADF uses a sense/loop combination. HF uses a wire antenna or notch antenna. The blade on the belly is the transponder/ADS-B antenna.

preassessment.question: What type of antenna does a transponder use?
choices: V-shaped dipole at 108–118 MHz
Loop at 190–535 kHz
Low-profile blade/stub at 1030/1090 MHz
Patch at 1575 MHz

questions.learningObjective: Mode C provides both squawk code and altitude simultaneously
questions.fullObjective: State that Mode C is the transponder mode that provides both aircraft identification (Mode A squawk code) and pressure altitude reporting simultaneously.

flashcard.term: Mode C — combined ID and altitude
flashcard.definition: Mode C is the standard operating mode for IFR flight, providing both Mode A squawk code (identity) and pressure altitude in a single transponder reply. Most ATC radar requires Mode C in controlled airspace above 10,000 MSL and near Class B/C airports.
flashcard.analogy: Like sending a package with both a name label AND a return address — ATC gets both who you are and where you are vertically in one reply.

jeopardy.question: This transponder mode simultaneously provides both aircraft identification (squawk code) and pressure altitude reporting in a single reply to ATC radar.
choices: Mode C
Mode S
Mode A
Mode 4
jeopardy.explanation: Mode C combines Mode A (squawk code) with pressure altitude reporting. Mode A alone gives identity; Mode C gives identity + altitude. Mode S adds data link and unique 24-bit addressing. Mode C is the standard requirement for most controlled US airspace.

multipleChoice.question: What combination of information does Mode C provide?
choices: GPS position and velocity only
Both the Mode A squawk code (identification) and pressure altitude reporting
Pressure altitude only, with no identification
Pilot-entered flight plan data and squawk code
multipleChoice.explanation: Mode C = Mode A identity (squawk code) + automatic pressure altitude from encoding altimeter. This combination is what allows ATC radar displays to show both the labeled aircraft identity and its altitude — the minimum information needed for basic radar separation services.

practice.question: A controller asks an aircraft to 'squawk 4521 with Mode C.' What will ATC see on their radar after the pilot complies?
choices: Only the squawk code 4521 with no altitude data
Only the pressure altitude with no identity label
Both the squawk code 4521 and the aircraft's pressure altitude displayed together on the radar return
GPS position coordinates replacing the squawk code on the display
practice.explanation: With Mode C active and squawk 4521 set, ATC will see the radar return labeled with the flight's identity (correlated to squawk 4521 via the flight plan) AND the pressure altitude from the encoding altimeter — both displayed together on the SSR track. This is the standard ATC surveillance picture.

preassessment.question: What does Mode C provide?
choices: GPS position and velocity
Altitude only
Flight plan data and squawk
Squawk code plus pressure altitude

questions.learningObjective: Impedance matching is most important factor for clear audio
questions.fullObjective: State that proper impedance matching between the audio panel output and connected avionics is the most important factor for achieving clear audio when integrating an audio panel with other avionics.

flashcard.definition: Audio signals must have matched impedance between the audio panel output and connected avionics inputs. Mismatched impedance causes signal reflections, reduced level, and distortion. Standard aviation audio interfaces are typically 150Ω or 600Ω — must match throughout the system.
flashcard.analogy: Like water pipes of different diameters at a junction — a mismatch creates back pressure (reflections) and reduces flow (signal level).

jeopardy.question: When integrating an audio panel with other avionics, this factor between audio panel output and connected equipment is the most important for achieving clear audio.
choices: RF shielding of audio cables
Impedance matching
Physical separation from the radio stack
Total cable length to each avionics unit
jeopardy.explanation: Impedance matching is the primary factor for audio quality in avionics integration. Mismatched impedances cause signal reflections, level loss, and distortion. All audio interfaces in the chain must have compatible impedances for clean signal transfer.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most important factor for clear audio when integrating an audio panel with avionics?
choices: Using the shortest possible cable runs to all connected radios
Installing an audio panel from the same manufacturer as the radios
Proper impedance matching between the audio panel output and connected avionics
Shielding all audio cables with ferrite chokes to prevent RF interference
multipleChoice.explanation: Impedance matching is the foundational requirement for audio system integration. All other factors (cable length, shielding, manufacturer) are secondary. A well-matched audio chain delivers clean signals; a mismatched one produces distortion, low level, or oscillation regardless of cable quality.

practice.question: After installing a new audio panel, the intercom has poor audio quality with distortion. All cable connections are verified correct. What should the tech check next?
choices: Replace the audio panel — distortion after installation indicates a defective unit
Increase the audio panel volume gain to overcome the distortion
Add ferrite chokes to all audio cables to reduce RF noise
Verify impedance matching between the audio panel output and the connected avionics audio inputs
practice.explanation: Post-installation audio distortion with verified wiring typically points to an impedance mismatch. The audio panel output impedance must match the input impedance of connected radios and intercom. Check the spec sheets for both the audio panel and connected avionics to verify compatibility.

preassessment.question: Most important factor for clear audio panel integration?
choices: Proper impedance matching
Short cable runs
Same manufacturer equipment
Ferrite choke shielding

questions.learningObjective: Audio panel routes comm/nav audio to pilot and copilot
questions.fullObjective: Explain the primary purpose of an audio panel in a general aviation aircraft: routing and selecting communication and navigation audio signals to the pilot and copilot headsets and cockpit speakers.

flashcard.definition: The audio panel routes and selects audio from multiple radios (COM1, COM2, NAV1, NAV2, ADF, marker beacon, etc.) to the pilot headset, copilot headset, and cabin speaker. Allows each crewmember to independently select what they hear. Also handles push-to-talk routing.
flashcard.analogy: Like a mixing board in a recording studio — multiple input sources, independently routed to different outputs (headsets/speakers).

jeopardy.question: Routing and selecting communication and navigation audio from multiple radios to pilot and copilot headsets and cockpit speakers is the primary purpose of this panel.
choices: The avionics master switch panel
The communication and navigation management unit
The audio panel
The interphone control unit
jeopardy.explanation: The audio panel is the audio routing hub: it takes inputs from all communication and navigation receivers and allows each crewmember to select what they want to monitor. It also routes PTT signals to the selected COM radio for transmission.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of an audio panel in a general aviation aircraft?
choices: Regulating electrical power to avionics from the main bus
Providing squelch control for all installed COM radios simultaneously
Combining GPS and VOR navigation data for display on the MFD
Routing and selecting comm and nav audio signals to pilot/copilot headsets and cockpit speakers
multipleChoice.explanation: The audio panel's core function is audio routing and selection — it receives audio from all installed comm and nav equipment and lets each crew position select which inputs to monitor. It also routes the outgoing mic/PTT signal to the selected transmitter.

practice.question: A pilot wants to monitor COM1 audio in the left headset while the right-seat passenger monitors NAV1 Morse ID audio. What enables this?
choices: The audio panel's independent pilot and copilot audio selection allows each position to select different audio sources
The aircraft's intercom system routes all audio equally to both headsets
Each radio has its own dedicated headset jack on the radio face
The audio panel automatically routes the most recently active radio to the left headset
practice.explanation: The audio panel provides independent audio selection for pilot and copilot positions — each selector independently routes chosen audio sources to that position's headset. This allows simultaneous monitoring of different sources at different crew stations, which is the primary value of a multi-channel audio panel.

preassessment.question: Primary purpose of an audio panel?
choices: Power regulation to avionics
Route and select comm/nav audio to headsets and speakers
Combine GPS and VOR for display
Simultaneous squelch control of all COMss

questions.learningObjective: Audio control panel enables crew to select and transmit on multiple radios
questions.fullObjective: State the primary purpose of an audio control panel: enabling the flight crew to select, monitor, and transmit on multiple communication and navigation radios from a single centralized control interface.

flashcard.definition: The Audio Control Panel (ACP) is the centralized interface for all aircraft audio: select which COM to transmit on, select which COM/NAV receivers to monitor (listen to), adjust intercom, and access PA (public address) system. All audio management from one panel.
flashcard.analogy: Like the switchboard at a telephone exchange — one central location to connect any input to any output.

jeopardy.question: A single centralized interface enabling crews to select, monitor, and transmit on multiple communication and navigation radios describes the primary purpose of this cockpit panel.
choices: Avionics master switch panel
Mode S transponder control head
Integrated avionics control unit
Audio control panel
jeopardy.explanation: The audio control panel centralizes all audio management: transmit selection (which COM to send on), receive selection (which COM/NAV/ADF to monitor), intercom control, and speaker selection — all from one panel accessible to both crew positions.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of an Audio Control Panel (ACP)?
choices: Enabling crew to select, monitor, and transmit on multiple comm and nav radios from one interface
Displaying traffic advisories from TCAS
Controlling the SELCAL alert system for oceanic operations only
Providing backup attitude reference when primary instruments fail
multipleChoice.explanation: The ACP centralizes audio management: choose which radio to transmit on (PTT routes to that COM), choose which radios/navs to listen to, and manage intercom and PA. This simplification of audio management reduces crew workload in complex multi-radio environments.

practice.question: A crew needs to simultaneously: pilot transmit on COM1, copilot monitor NAV2, and both monitor COM2. Which component makes this possible?
choices: The COM1 radio — it has a built-in audio matrix for two crew positions
The Audio Control Panel — it provides independent transmit and receive selection for each crew position across all installed radios
The avionics master bus — it routes audio automatically based on radio selection
The aircraft intercom — it handles all audio routing between crew positions
practice.explanation: Only the Audio Control Panel provides the routing matrix needed: pilot PTT routed to COM1, copilot's receive selector set to NAV2, both positions' receive selectors include COM2 for monitoring. This is exactly the ACP's design purpose — centralized independent audio management for multi-radio cockpit environments.

preassessment.question: Primary purpose of the Audio Control Panel?
choices: Display TCAS traffic
SELCAL alerts for oceanic ops only
Select, monitor, and transmit on multiple radios from one centralized interface
Backup attitude reference

questions.learningObjective: ADC as source of airspeed and altitude for PFD
questions.fullObjective: State that the Air Data Computer (ADC), connected to the pitot-static system, is the source of airspeed and altitude data for the Primary Flight Display in a glass-cockpit system.

flashcard.definition: A computer connected to the pitot-static system that processes ram air and static pressure to calculate airspeed and altitude data, supplying this information to the Primary Flight Display in a glass-cockpit system.
flashcard.analogy: The ADC is like a translator — it converts raw air pressures into digital numbers the PFD screen can display.

jeopardy.question: In a glass-cockpit aircraft, this computer processes pitot-static pressure data to supply airspeed and altitude information to the Primary Flight Display.
choices: Air Data Computer (ADC)
GPS receiver
Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS)
Flight Management System (FMS)
jeopardy.explanation: The ADC interfaces directly with the pitot-static system and converts pressure measurements into digital airspeed and altitude data for the PFD.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary function of the Air Data Computer in a glass-cockpit avionics suite?
choices: Display navigation data on the moving map
Convert pitot-static pressure inputs into digital airspeed and altitude for the PFD
Provide GPS position updates to the flight plan
Drive the attitude indicator using gyroscopic data
multipleChoice.explanation: The ADC is connected to the pitot-static system and computes airspeed and altitude from pressure inputs, then outputs this data to the PFD.

practice.question: A pilot reports that airspeed and altitude data have disappeared from the PFD. The GPS and attitude displays are still functioning normally. Which LRU should the avionics technician suspect first?
choices: AHRS unit
GPS/Nav receiver
Air Data Computer (ADC)
Display Unit (DU)
practice.explanation: Since GPS and attitude (AHRS) are normal but airspeed/altitude are missing, the ADC — which processes pitot-static data for the PFD — is the prime suspect.

preassessment.question: In a glass-cockpit system, which unit supplies airspeed and altitude data to the Primary Flight Display?
choices: AHRS
MFD
FMS
ADC

questions.learningObjective: EFIS uses MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers for attitude data
questions.fullObjective: State that an EFIS typically uses solid-state sensors including MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers to measure aircraft attitude, heading, and motion without moving parts.

flashcard.definition: Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) gyroscopes and accelerometers used in Electronic Flight Instrument Systems to measure attitude, heading, and motion without moving mechanical parts.
flashcard.analogy: MEMS sensors are like motion sensors in a smartphone — tiny, solid-state chips that detect movement and orientation without any spinning parts.

jeopardy.question: These solid-state devices, used in modern EFIS attitude sensing, measure angular rate and linear acceleration without any rotating or moving mechanical components.
choices: Laser ring gyroscopes only
MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers
Vacuum-driven spinning gyro rotors
Flux gate compass sensors
jeopardy.explanation: EFIS systems use MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) sensors — solid-state gyroscopes and accelerometers — to determine attitude and motion without mechanical moving parts.

multipleChoice.question: What type of sensors does a modern EFIS typically use to determine aircraft attitude and heading?
choices: Mechanical gyro rotors driven by engine vacuum
GPS antennas measuring position change over time
Solid-state MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers
Pitot-static pressure transducers
multipleChoice.explanation: Modern EFIS attitude systems rely on solid-state MEMS technology — tiny gyroscopes and accelerometers on microchips — eliminating moving mechanical parts.

practice.question: A technician is explaining to a student why a modern glass-cockpit AHRS does not require a vacuum pump to operate. Which statement best explains this?
choices: The AHRS uses GPS updates to determine attitude
The AHRS relies on the aircraft's inertial navigation system
The AHRS is driven electrically by a brushless motor
The AHRS uses solid-state MEMS sensors that have no spinning mechanical parts requiring air-driven power
practice.explanation: Unlike vacuum-driven spinning gyros, MEMS-based AHRS sensors are solid-state — they measure motion electronically with no moving parts, so no vacuum pump is needed.

preassessment.question: Which sensor type does an EFIS use to measure attitude without mechanical moving parts?
choices: MEMS sensors
Vacuum gyros
Pitot tubes
Flux valves

questions.learningObjective: Traditional instruments replaced by modern PFD
questions.fullObjective: List the traditional flight instruments replaced by a modern glass-cockpit PFD: airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, vertical speed indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator.

flashcard.definition: A modern Primary Flight Display (PFD) replaces six traditional instruments: airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, vertical speed indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator.
flashcard.analogy: The PFD is like a smartphone replacing a watch, calculator, calendar, camera, map, and compass — six devices consolidated into one screen.

jeopardy.question: These six traditional round-dial instruments are consolidated onto a single Primary Flight Display in a glass-cockpit aircraft.
choices: ASI, ADI, altimeter, DME, DI, turn coordinator
ASI, ADI, altimeter, VSI, DI, ADF
ASI, ADI, altimeter, VSI, DI, turn coordinator
ASI, ADI, altimeter, VOR, DI, HSI
jeopardy.explanation: The PFD consolidates the ASI (airspeed), ADI (attitude), altimeter, VSI (vertical speed), DI (heading/directional gyro), and turn coordinator into one integrated display.

multipleChoice.question: Which set of instruments does a modern glass-cockpit Primary Flight Display replace?
choices: Airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, GPS, MFD, TCAS
Airspeed indicator, altimeter, VOR, ADF, heading indicator, turn coordinator
Attitude indicator, altimeter, DME, VSI, GPS, turn coordinator
Airspeed indicator, VSI, altimeter, attitude indicator, heading indicator, turn coordinator
multipleChoice.explanation: The PFD replaces the six classic 'six-pack' instruments: airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, VSI, heading indicator (DI), and turn coordinator.

practice.question: During a retrofit, a customer asks which traditional cockpit instruments will become redundant after a glass-cockpit PFD is installed. Which response is most accurate?
choices: The airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, VSI, heading indicator, and turn coordinator are all replaced by the PFD
Only the attitude indicator and altimeter are replaced; the others remain
The PFD replaces only navigation instruments like VOR and ADF
The PFD replaces engine instruments and the HSI
practice.explanation: A PFD integrates all six primary flight instruments (ASI, ADI, ALT, VSI, DI, TC) into one display, making all six traditional round dials redundant.

preassessment.question: How many traditional flight instruments does a glass-cockpit PFD replace?
choices: Three
Six
Five
Four

questions.learningObjective: Altimeter must be set to local barometric pressure before flight
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the altimeter must be set to the current local barometric pressure (altimeter setting in inches of mercury) before flight to indicate accurate altitude above sea level.

flashcard.definition: The local barometric pressure in inches of mercury (inHg) entered into the altimeter's Kollsman window before flight so the instrument reads accurate altitude above mean sea level.
flashcard.analogy: Setting the altimeter is like zeroing a bathroom scale before weighing — without the correct reference baseline, every reading will be off by the same constant error.

jeopardy.question: Before flight, this value in inches of mercury must be entered in the altimeter's Kollsman window so the instrument correctly reads altitude above sea level.
choices: Standard pressure of 29.92 inHg always
Ram air pressure from the pitot tube
The airport elevation in feet
Current local barometric pressure (altimeter setting)
jeopardy.explanation: The local altimeter setting (QNH) is dialed into the Kollsman window to reference the altimeter to local atmospheric pressure, giving an accurate MSL altitude reading.

multipleChoice.question: Why must the altimeter be set to the current local barometric pressure before flight?
choices: So the altimeter reads accurate altitude above sea level referenced to local atmospheric conditions
To prevent the static port from becoming blocked at altitude
To synchronize the altimeter with the air data computer
To set the transition altitude for pressurized aircraft
multipleChoice.explanation: Without the correct local pressure setting, the altimeter references an incorrect baseline, causing altitude errors equal to approximately 1,000 ft per inch of mercury difference.

practice.question: A pilot reports that, after landing at an airport with published elevation 500 ft MSL, the altimeter reads 650 ft with the Kollsman window set to 29.92 inHg. The current local altimeter setting is 29.77 inHg. What explains the 150 ft discrepancy?
choices: The static port is partially blocked, adding pressure to the system
The altimeter is set to standard pressure rather than local QNH, causing an over-read of approximately 150 ft
The pitot tube is blocked, preventing accurate pressure measurement
The altimeter calibration is shifted and requires bench calibration
practice.explanation: Each 0.10 inHg error in altimeter setting causes approximately 100 ft of altitude error. Using 29.92 instead of 29.77 (a 0.15 inHg high setting) produces a ~150 ft high reading.

preassessment.question: What must be entered in the altimeter's Kollsman window before flight for accurate MSL altitude?
choices: Airport elevation
Standard 29.92 inHg always
Local barometric pressure
Ram air pressure

questions.learningObjective: Vacuum system spins gyro rotor via air through nozzle
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the vacuum system in a vacuum-driven attitude indicator spins the gyro rotor by drawing air through a nozzle that impinges on the rotor buckets, providing the power source for the instrument.

flashcard.definition: In a vacuum-driven attitude indicator, suction created by an engine-driven vacuum pump draws filtered air through a nozzle; the air jet impinges on buckets around the gyro rotor rim, spinning it at high RPM.
flashcard.analogy: It works like a water wheel — instead of water falling on paddles, a jet of air blows on rotor buckets to make the gyro spin fast enough to maintain rigidity.

jeopardy.question: In a vacuum-driven attitude indicator, air is drawn through this component by the vacuum system and directed at rotor buckets to spin the gyro.
choices: A nozzle
A centrifugal impeller
A venturi tube mounted externally
An electric motor armature
jeopardy.explanation: The vacuum system draws filtered air through a nozzle aimed at buckets machined around the rotor rim; the jet of air impinges on the buckets to spin the gyro.

multipleChoice.question: How does the vacuum system provide power to spin the gyro rotor in an air-driven attitude indicator?
choices: Positive pressure from the engine blower forces air directly into the gyro case
Engine vacuum draws air through a nozzle that directs a jet against rotor buckets, spinning the rotor
A venturi tube in the airstream creates suction to rotate a turbine connected to the rotor
Electrical solenoids modulate airflow to vary gyro speed as needed
multipleChoice.explanation: An engine-driven vacuum pump creates suction that draws air through a nozzle; the air jet impinges on bucket-shaped cutouts on the rotor periphery, spinning the gyro.

practice.question: A technician notices that the attitude indicator is precessing (slowly tumbling) during flight. Vacuum gauge reads 3.5 inHg when the spec is 4.8–5.3 inHg. What is the most likely cause of the instrument precession?
choices: The static port is blocked, reducing airflow to the nozzle
The gyro gimbal bearings have worn and allow excessive precession even at correct vacuum
Insufficient vacuum pressure means the air jet cannot spin the gyro rotor to the required RPM for rigidity
Incorrect altimeter setting is causing feedback into the attitude display
practice.explanation: Gyroscopic rigidity depends on rotor speed (RPM). Low vacuum means lower airflow through the nozzle and lower rotor speed, reducing rigidity and allowing precession.

preassessment.question: What drives the gyro rotor in a vacuum-driven attitude indicator?
choices: Electric motor
Ram air pressure
Engine-driven belt
Air jets through a nozzle

questions.learningObjective: Blocked pitot tube primarily affects airspeed indicator
questions.fullObjective: Given a completely blocked pitot tube, determine that the airspeed indicator is the instrument primarily affected because it relies on ram air pressure that is no longer available.

flashcard.definition: A completely blocked pitot tube removes ram air pressure input to the airspeed indicator; the ASI is the primary instrument affected because it requires the differential between ram pressure and static pressure to compute airspeed.
flashcard.analogy: Blocking the pitot tube is like covering one end of a differential pressure gauge — you lose the 'high side' reference, so the measurement becomes meaningless.

jeopardy.question: When a pitot tube becomes completely blocked, this flight instrument is primarily affected because it needs ram air pressure to calculate airspeed.
choices: Altimeter
Airspeed indicator
Vertical speed indicator
Attitude indicator
jeopardy.explanation: The ASI uses the differential between ram (pitot) pressure and static pressure. A blocked pitot tube eliminates the ram pressure input, making the ASI unable to measure airspeed.

multipleChoice.question: A pitot tube becomes completely blocked in flight. Which instrument is most directly and immediately affected?
choices: Altimeter, because it also uses the pitot line for reference
Vertical speed indicator, because rate of climb depends on pitot pressure
Airspeed indicator, because it depends on ram air pressure that is now unavailable
Turn coordinator, because it uses pitot air to drive the gyro rotor
multipleChoice.explanation: Only the ASI uses the pitot (ram pressure) line. The altimeter and VSI use only the static line. The turn coordinator is typically electrically or vacuum driven.

practice.question: During preflight, a technician covers the pitot tube with tape to prevent contamination. The tape is inadvertently left on during flight. Which instrument indication would alert the pilot to this problem first?
choices: The altimeter freezes at a fixed altitude reading
The attitude indicator tumbles due to loss of air-driven power
The VSI shows a constant zero regardless of actual climb
The airspeed indicator shows zero or an abnormally fixed reading
practice.explanation: The pitot tube provides ram pressure only to the ASI (and ADC/airspeed circuits). With the pitot blocked, the ASI will read zero or remain frozen, alerting the pilot immediately.

preassessment.question: Which instrument is primarily affected by a completely blocked pitot tube?
choices: Airspeed indicator
Altimeter
VSI
Heading indicator

questions.learningObjective: Turn coordinator senses roll rate and yaw rate; turn-and-bank senses only yaw rate
questions.fullObjective: Distinguish between a turn coordinator and a turn-and-bank indicator: the turn coordinator senses both roll rate and yaw rate, while the turn-and-bank only senses yaw rate.

flashcard.definition: The turn coordinator's gyro is canted at approximately 30° to sense both roll rate and yaw rate; the turn-and-bank indicator's gyro is vertical (upright), sensing yaw rate only.
flashcard.analogy: Think of tilting a steering wheel at an angle — a canted wheel can detect turning AND leaning motions, while a straight-up wheel only detects turning.

jeopardy.question: Unlike the turn-and-bank indicator, this instrument senses both roll rate and yaw rate because its gyro is canted at approximately 30 degrees.
choices: Attitude indicator
Directional gyro
Turn coordinator
Heading indicator
jeopardy.explanation: The turn coordinator's gyro is tilted at ~30° so it responds to both yaw and roll. The turn-and-bank indicator has a vertically mounted gyro that only senses yaw.

multipleChoice.question: What is the key functional difference between a turn coordinator and a turn-and-bank indicator?
choices: The turn coordinator is electrically driven while the turn-and-bank is vacuum driven
The turn coordinator uses a ball inclinometer while the turn-and-bank does not
The turn-and-bank shows coordinated flight while the turn coordinator only shows turn direction
The turn coordinator senses both roll and yaw rate; the turn-and-bank senses yaw rate only
multipleChoice.explanation: The critical difference is gyro orientation: the turn coordinator's canted gyro senses roll rate in addition to yaw, while the turn-and-bank's vertical gyro senses only yaw rate.

practice.question: An aircraft has a serviceable slip/skid ball but a failed turn coordinator. The pilot notes the miniature aircraft in the TC shows no movement at all during a coordinated turn. The technician confirms normal ball deflection during the turn. Which statement best describes this situation?
choices: The turn coordinator gyro has failed (no yaw/roll sensing), but the ball inclinometer is a separate gravity-pendulum device unaffected by the gyro failure
Both the turn coordinator and inclinometer ball use the same gyro, so neither should work
The ball is also failed but masked by viscosity of the dampening fluid
A vacuum system failure is causing both instruments to fail in different ways
practice.explanation: The inclinometer ball is a gravity-pendulum instrument that operates independently of the gyro. A failed TC gyro leaves the ball functional — the ball and gyro are separate mechanisms.

preassessment.question: Which rates does the turn coordinator's gyro sense due to its canted mounting?
choices: Yaw only
Roll and yaw
Pitch and yaw
Roll only

questions.learningObjective: Magnetic compass is not gyroscopically driven
questions.fullObjective: State that the magnetic compass is the flight instrument that is not gyroscopically driven, relying instead on Earth's magnetic field for directional reference.

flashcard.definition: The only primary flight instrument not gyroscopically driven; it uses Earth's magnetic field to align a magnetic sensing element and provide directional reference.
flashcard.analogy: A magnetic compass is like a trail of iron filings aligning to a bar magnet — no motor or spin needed, just the Earth's natural magnetic field.

jeopardy.question: Among primary flight instruments, this directional reference instrument requires no gyro or power source because it aligns directly to Earth's magnetic field.
choices: Heading indicator (DI)
Turn coordinator
HSI (Horizontal Situation Indicator)
Magnetic compass
jeopardy.explanation: The magnetic compass uses Earth's magnetic field — not a gyro. It works without electrical or vacuum power, making it the ultimate backup directional reference.

multipleChoice.question: Which primary flight instrument does NOT rely on gyroscopic principles for its operation?
choices: Magnetic compass
Heading indicator (directional gyro)
Attitude indicator
Turn coordinator
multipleChoice.explanation: The magnetic compass operates on magnetic principles — it aligns to Earth's magnetic field. All the others (AI, DI, TC) use spinning gyroscopes.

practice.question: All electrical and vacuum power is lost in flight. Which primary flight instruments remain functional?
choices: Attitude indicator and heading indicator, as they are vacuum-driven
Only the magnetic compass and pitot-static instruments (altimeter, ASI, VSI), which require no power
Only the GPS-driven heading display on the EFIS
No instruments remain functional without electrical power
practice.explanation: Pitot-static instruments (altimeter, ASI, VSI) are purely mechanical/pneumatic — no power needed. The magnetic compass is self-powered by Earth's field. These all work in a complete power failure.

preassessment.question: Which flight instrument operates using Earth's magnetic field rather than a gyroscope?
choices: Attitude indicator
Heading indicator
Magnetic compass
Turn coordinator

questions.learningObjective: Blocked pitot during descent causes falsely high ASI reading
questions.fullObjective: Predict that a blocked pitot tube during descent will cause the airspeed indicator to read falsely high because the trapped pressure does not decrease as altitude decreases.

flashcard.definition: During descent with a blocked pitot tube, static pressure increases while the trapped ram pressure stays constant; the ASI reads the increasing differential as increasing airspeed, showing a falsely high and increasing reading.
flashcard.analogy: Imagine a sealed container being pushed deeper underwater — the outside pressure rises but the inside stays the same, making the pressure difference appear larger than reality.

jeopardy.question: During descent, a blocked pitot tube causes the ASI to read falsely high because static pressure increases while this pressure remains trapped and constant.
choices: Ram (impact) air pressure
Dynamic pressure
Cabin differential pressure
Total pressure
jeopardy.explanation: In descent, static port pressure rises. The trapped ram pressure stays constant. The ASI sees an increasing pitot-minus-static differential and registers a falsely high airspeed.

multipleChoice.question: An aircraft descends with a completely blocked pitot tube. What will the airspeed indicator show?
choices: A correctly decreasing airspeed consistent with actual speed
A falsely high and increasing airspeed as static pressure rises while pitot pressure remains trapped
Zero airspeed because no ram air pressure is available
An airspeed equal to stall speed due to minimum differential pressure
multipleChoice.explanation: During descent, atmospheric (static) pressure increases. Because the pitot is blocked, ram pressure is trapped at the level where the blockage occurred. The ASI senses a growing differential, indicating falsely increasing airspeed.

practice.question: A test pilot reports that the ASI showed steadily increasing speed during a carefully controlled constant-speed descent. Actual aircraft speed was verified stable by radar tracking. What pitot-static fault best explains this discrepancy?
choices: Blocked static port, causing static pressure to remain fixed while pitot pressure rises
A leak in the pitot line, causing the ASI to reference ambient pressure on both sides
Blocked pitot tube, causing trapped ram pressure to remain constant while rising static pressure creates a growing differential
An ADC calibration error resulting in a fixed offset throughout the descent
practice.explanation: A blocked pitot traps ram pressure at the blockage level. Descending raises static pressure, increasing the pitot-minus-static differential, so the ASI reads a falsely high and rising airspeed.

preassessment.question: What ASI indication results from a blocked pitot tube during descent?
choices: Zero airspeed
Correctly decreasing airspeed
Constant fixed airspeed
Falsely high and rising airspeed

questions.learningObjective: VSI displays rate of change of barometric altitude
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the Vertical Speed Indicator (VSI) displays the rate of change of barometric altitude in feet per minute, allowing the pilot to monitor climb and descent rate.

flashcard.definition: A flight instrument that measures and displays the rate of change of barometric altitude in feet per minute (fpm) by comparing the static pressure entering a chamber with a calibrated leak rate.
flashcard.analogy: A VSI is like a pressure change detector — it doesn't read pressure, it reads how fast pressure is changing, which corresponds to how fast you're climbing or descending.

jeopardy.question: This pitot-static instrument displays the rate of change of barometric altitude in feet per minute by sensing how quickly static pressure is changing.
choices: Altimeter
Vertical Speed Indicator (VSI)
Airspeed indicator
Air data computer
jeopardy.explanation: The VSI uses a differential between a direct static port connection and a calibrated metering orifice to measure how fast static pressure is changing, giving a climb/descent rate in fpm.

multipleChoice.question: What does the Vertical Speed Indicator measure and how is it expressed?
choices: Absolute altitude above sea level, expressed in feet MSL
Dynamic pressure change, expressed in knots per minute
Rate of change of barometric altitude, expressed in feet per minute
Engine climb power setting, expressed in percent RPM
multipleChoice.explanation: The VSI measures the rate at which static pressure is changing and converts this into a vertical rate reading in feet per minute — positive for climb, negative for descent.

practice.question: During a steady, unaccelerated climb at 500 fpm, the VSI reads 0 fpm. The altimeter and ASI appear normal. Which fault is most consistent with a stuck VSI at zero?
choices: A blocked pitot tube preventing airspeed measurement from reaching the VSI
A vacuum pump failure preventing the gyro from spinning
An ADC failure causing altitude data to freeze on the PFD
A blockage in the static port or the VSI's calibrated metering orifice preventing pressure change detection
practice.explanation: The VSI needs to sense a changing pressure differential through its calibrated orifice. A blockage in the static line or orifice traps static pressure, preventing any differential — the needle stays at zero.

preassessment.question: In what units does the Vertical Speed Indicator display rate of climb or descent?
choices: Feet per minute
Feet per second
Knots
Meters per minute

questions.learningObjective: Blocked static port freezes altimeter and VSI at zero
questions.fullObjective: Predict the effect of a blocked static port on instruments: the altimeter will freeze at the altitude where the port was blocked, and the VSI will read zero regardless of actual aircraft movement.

flashcard.definition: A blocked static port traps pressure at the level where blockage occurred, causing the altimeter to freeze at that altitude and the VSI to read zero, because neither can detect changes in atmospheric pressure.
flashcard.analogy: It's like sealing a barometer inside a closed box — the pressure inside stays constant regardless of what happens outside, so you'll never see a change.

jeopardy.question: A blocked static port causes these two instruments to become unusable: one freezes at the altitude where the blockage occurred, and the other reads zero continuously.
choices: ASI and turn coordinator
Attitude indicator and heading indicator
Altimeter and VSI
VSI and airspeed indicator
jeopardy.explanation: Both the altimeter and VSI rely on static pressure changes. A blocked static port traps pressure — the altimeter freezes at the blocked altitude, and the VSI reads zero (no pressure change detected).

multipleChoice.question: What happens to the altimeter and VSI when the static port becomes completely blocked?
choices: The altimeter reads falsely high and the VSI reads maximum climb
The altimeter reads zero and the VSI shows maximum descent
Both instruments read the altitude from the last GPS fix
The altimeter freezes at the altitude where blockage occurred, and the VSI reads zero
multipleChoice.explanation: Both instruments require changing static pressure to function. A blocked port traps a fixed pressure, so the altimeter needle freezes and the VSI shows zero regardless of actual altitude changes.

practice.question: During a climb from 2,000 ft to 8,000 ft, the altimeter stays fixed at 2,000 ft and the VSI reads zero throughout. The ASI reads correctly. What fault is most consistent with these indications?
choices: A blocked static port trapping the static pressure at the level where the blockage occurred (2,000 ft)
A blocked pitot tube preventing ram air from reaching pressure-sensing instruments
A failed ADC causing both altitude and vertical speed outputs to freeze
An altimeter calibration fault coincidentally correlated with a failed VSI spring
practice.explanation: The ASI working normally rules out a pitot blockage. The altimeter frozen at takeoff altitude and VSI at zero — with no change during actual climb — is the classic symptom of a blocked static port.

preassessment.question: Which instruments are most affected by a blocked static port?
choices: ASI and turn coordinator
Altimeter and VSI
Heading indicator and ADI
VSI and ASI

questions.learningObjective: Attitude indicator and DG are vacuum-powered
questions.fullObjective: State that the attitude indicator and directional gyro (heading indicator) are the flight instruments commonly powered by the aircraft vacuum system.

flashcard.definition: The attitude indicator (AI) and directional gyro (DG/heading indicator) are the two primary flight instruments that rely on the aircraft vacuum system to spin their gyro rotors.
flashcard.analogy: Think of the vacuum pump as a fan that keeps two spinning tops (the AI and DG gyros) spinning fast enough to stay upright and point in a fixed direction.

jeopardy.question: These two flight instruments are commonly powered by the aircraft vacuum system, which spins their gyro rotors via air jets.
choices: Airspeed indicator and altimeter
VSI and altimeter
Turn coordinator and magnetic compass
Attitude indicator and directional gyro
jeopardy.explanation: The AI and DG (heading indicator) are conventionally vacuum-driven. The ASI, altimeter, and VSI are pitot-static; the TC is typically electrically driven.

multipleChoice.question: Which two flight instruments are commonly powered by the aircraft vacuum system?
choices: Attitude indicator and directional gyro (heading indicator)
Airspeed indicator and altimeter
Turn coordinator and VSI
Magnetic compass and attitude indicator
multipleChoice.explanation: The attitude indicator and DG use vacuum-driven gyros. The pitot-static instruments (ASI, ALT, VSI) use air pressure, and the TC is usually electric.

practice.question: The vacuum gauge in an aircraft shows a complete vacuum pump failure. Which two flight instruments will the technician identify as now unreliable?
choices: Airspeed indicator and altimeter — they depend on the vacuum pump for pressure sensing
Attitude indicator and directional gyro — their gyros will lose speed and become unreliable
Turn coordinator and VSI — both are gyro instruments powered by vacuum
Magnetic compass and attitude indicator — both use vacuum for alignment
practice.explanation: The AI and DG (heading indicator) are vacuum-driven. Without vacuum, their rotors slow down and the instruments become unreliable. The ASI, ALT, and VSI are pitot-static and unaffected.

preassessment.question: Which instruments lose reliability when the vacuum pump fails?
choices: ASI and altimeter
Turn coordinator and VSI
Attitude indicator and DG
Compass and VSI

questions.learningObjective: Blocked pitot during climb causes falsely increasing ASI reading
questions.fullObjective: Predict that a blocked pitot tube during climb will cause the airspeed indicator to show a falsely increasing reading because the trapped ram air pressure does not decrease with altitude.

flashcard.definition: During a climb with a blocked pitot tube, static pressure decreases while the trapped ram pressure stays constant; the ASI sees a growing pitot-minus-static differential and reads a falsely increasing airspeed.
flashcard.analogy: Like a sealed balloon rising — the outside pressure drops but the inside pressure stays the same, creating an increasing pressure difference that the ASI misinterprets as speed.

jeopardy.question: During a climb with a blocked pitot tube, static pressure decreases while this pressure stays constant, causing the ASI to show falsely increasing airspeed.
choices: Ram air pressure (trapped)
Dynamic pressure
Cabin pressure
Manifold pressure
jeopardy.explanation: During climb, atmospheric pressure falls. Blocked pitot traps ram pressure constant. ASI measures pitot minus static — as static decreases, the differential grows, reading falsely high airspeed.

multipleChoice.question: An aircraft climbs with a completely blocked pitot tube. What will the airspeed indicator show?
choices: A correctly increasing airspeed consistent with actual aircraft speed
A falsely increasing airspeed because static pressure falls while trapped ram pressure stays constant
A falsely decreasing airspeed as static pressure falls
Zero airspeed because the pitot tube cannot sense any air movement
multipleChoice.explanation: In a climb, atmospheric pressure (static) falls. The blocked pitot traps constant ram pressure. The ASI measures the growing differential (constant pitot minus decreasing static) as increasing airspeed.

practice.question: During an IFR climb in icing conditions, a pilot reports the airspeed increasing even though power was not changed and the aircraft pitch was held constant. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The VSI is showing false data due to a blocked static port
The altimeter has frozen causing erroneous cross-checks with the ASI
Ice has blocked the pitot tube; as altitude increases, static pressure drops creating a falsely increasing ASI reading
An ADC software error is adding a constant offset to computed airspeed
practice.explanation: Ice blockage of the pitot tube during climb in icing conditions is a common accident cause. The trapped ram pressure creates an increasing differential with falling static pressure, showing false increasing airspeed.

preassessment.question: What does the ASI show during a climb with a blocked pitot tube?
choices: Correctly increasing
Falsely decreasing
Fixed at zero
Falsely increasing

questions.learningObjective: AHRS uses rate gyros, accelerometers, magnetometers — no mechanical gyros
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the AHRS in an EFIS uses solid-state sensors including rate gyroscopes, accelerometers, and magnetometers to determine aircraft attitude, heading, and angular rates without spinning mechanical gyros.

flashcard.definition: An Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS) uses three types of solid-state sensors: rate gyroscopes (angular rate), accelerometers (linear acceleration), and magnetometers (magnetic heading) — no spinning mechanical gyros.
flashcard.analogy: An AHRS is like your inner ear combined with a compass — rate gyros detect rotation, accelerometers detect tilting/movement, and magnetometers detect magnetic north, all without moving parts.

jeopardy.question: The AHRS in a modern EFIS determines attitude, heading, and angular rates using these three types of solid-state sensors instead of spinning mechanical gyros.
choices: GPS receivers, barometric sensors, and radar altimeters
Rate gyroscopes, accelerometers, and magnetometers
Pitot probes, static ports, and angle-of-attack vanes
MEMS chips, ADC units, and flux gate compasses only
jeopardy.explanation: AHRS systems combine rate gyros (angular rate), accelerometers (linear motion/gravity), and magnetometers (magnetic heading) in solid-state form — no mechanical spinning gyros needed.

multipleChoice.question: What three types of solid-state sensors does an AHRS use to replace traditional mechanical gyroscopes?
choices: Pitot probes, static vents, and angle-of-attack sensors
GPS receivers, barometric sensors, and ADS-B transceivers
Rate gyroscopes, accelerometers, and magnetometers
MEMS gyros, strain gauges, and flux gate detectors
multipleChoice.explanation: A complete AHRS sensor suite uses rate gyroscopes for angular rate, accelerometers for linear acceleration and tilt, and magnetometers for magnetic heading reference.

practice.question: After an AHRS replacement on an EFIS-equipped aircraft, the heading indication drifts significantly after power-up but attitude appears correct. Which sensor within the AHRS is most likely faulty or requires calibration?
choices: The rate gyroscopes, which control attitude but not heading
The GPS receiver, which provides position but not heading
The accelerometers, which measure tilt to establish a gravity reference
The magnetometer, which provides magnetic heading reference
practice.explanation: The magnetometer is the AHRS component that provides magnetic heading. If attitude (rate gyros + accelerometers) is correct but heading drifts, the magnetometer or its calibration is suspect.

preassessment.question: Which three sensor types are used in a modern AHRS?
choices: Rate gyros, accelerometers, magnetometers
GPS, pitot, altimeter
ADC, FMS, transponder
VSI, ASI, altimeter

questions.learningObjective: Consistently low ASI reading indicates partial pitot blockage or leak
questions.fullObjective: Given an airspeed indicator consistently reading too low, determine that a partial pitot tube blockage or leak in the pitot line reducing ram air pressure is the most likely cause.

flashcard.definition: An airspeed indicator that consistently reads below actual airspeed indicates reduced ram air pressure reaching the ASI, which can result from a partial pitot tube blockage or a leak in the pitot pressure plumbing.
flashcard.analogy: Like a kinked garden hose — a partial blockage or hole reduces the pressure reaching the end, so you measure less force than is actually there.

jeopardy.question: An airspeed indicator that consistently reads lower than actual aircraft speed most likely has this type of fault in the pitot pressure system.
choices: Blocked static port
Over-pressurized pitot system
Partial pitot tube blockage or leak in pitot plumbing
Defective VSI orifice
jeopardy.explanation: A consistent low reading means less ram pressure is reaching the ASI than expected. A partial blockage (reduced flow) or a leak (pressure escaping) in the pitot line both reduce effective ram pressure.

multipleChoice.question: An airspeed indicator consistently reads 15 knots below actual aircraft speed verified by other means. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The static port is fully blocked, reducing reference pressure
The aircraft's true airspeed correction is not applied at this altitude
The altimeter calibration error is cross-affecting the ASI reading
A partial pitot blockage or a leak in the pitot pressure line is reducing the ram air pressure at the ASI
multipleChoice.explanation: Consistent under-reading means reduced ram pressure at the ASI. A partial pitot blockage reduces flow, and a leak allows some pressure to escape — both produce a persistently low reading.

practice.question: During a test flight, an avionics technician notes the ASI consistently reads 12 knots low across all speeds compared to a calibrated reference. The altimeter and VSI are normal. What should the technician inspect?
choices: The pitot tube for partial obstruction and all pitot plumbing for cracks, loose fittings, or moisture traps causing leaks
The static port for full or partial blockage reducing reference pressure
The altimeter capsule for a calibration shift affecting shared components
The turn coordinator gyro for drag affecting airspeed through increased resistance
practice.explanation: With altimeter and VSI normal, the static system is intact. A consistent low ASI points to reduced ram pressure — inspect the pitot tube for partial blockage and the pitot plumbing for leaks.

preassessment.question: An ASI consistently reading too low suggests which type of fault?
choices: Blocked static port
Partial pitot blockage or pitot line leak
Overpressure in the static line
Altimeter calibration error

questions.learningObjective: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inHg
questions.fullObjective: State that standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inches of mercury (1013.25 millibars or hectopascals).

flashcard.definition: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inches of mercury (inHg), equivalent to 1013.25 millibars (mb) or hectopascals (hPa).
flashcard.analogy: Think of 29.92 inHg as the 'sea level zero' reference — like setting a measuring tape to start at zero before measuring distance from the ground.

jeopardy.question: This value — 29.92 inches of mercury or 1013.25 millibars — represents the standard atmospheric pressure at sea level used as a reference in aviation.
choices: The minimum safe pressure for pressurized cabins
The altimeter correction factor for high-altitude flight
The maximum pitot static test pressure limit
Standard sea level atmospheric pressure
jeopardy.explanation: 29.92 inHg (1013.25 mb/hPa) is the International Standard Atmosphere sea-level pressure. Altimeters set to this value read pressure altitude rather than true altitude.

multipleChoice.question: What is the standard atmospheric pressure at sea level in the International Standard Atmosphere?
multipleChoice.explanation: The ISA defines sea-level standard pressure as 29.92 inHg or 1013.25 mb/hPa. This is the reference for pressure altitude and standard altimeter setting (FL above transition altitude).

practice.question: A technician performing a pitot-static system certification test needs to input the standard altimeter setting to check the altimeter reading at a simulated sea level condition. What value should be entered?
choices: 28.00 inHg — the minimum pressure seen at sea level
29.92 inHg — the ISA standard sea-level pressure used as the reference
30.50 inHg — the average pressure at the test facility
The current field altimeter setting from the ATIS
practice.explanation: 29.92 inHg is the ISA standard. When set to 29.92, the altimeter reads pressure altitude referenced to standard sea level — the correct reference for test and certification.

preassessment.question: What is standard sea-level atmospheric pressure?
choices: 28.86 inHg
30.15 inHg
29.92 inHg
31.00 inHg

questions.learningObjective: Pitot tube senses and delivers ram air pressure to airspeed indicator
questions.fullObjective: State that the primary function of the pitot tube is to sense and deliver ram air pressure to the airspeed indicator and air data systems.

flashcard.definition: The pitot tube is an open-faced probe pointed into the airstream to sense and collect ram (impact) air pressure, which it delivers to the airspeed indicator and air data computer for airspeed computation.
flashcard.analogy: A pitot tube is like holding a tube into the wind — the faster the wind blows, the more pressure builds up inside, and that pressure is measured to determine speed.

jeopardy.question: This forward-facing probe senses and delivers ram air pressure — the key input to the airspeed indicator and air data computer.
choices: Pitot tube
Angle-of-attack vane
Static port
Total air temperature probe
jeopardy.explanation: The pitot tube collects ram (impact) air pressure and delivers it to the ASI and ADC. The pressure increases with airspeed, enabling airspeed computation.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary function of the pitot tube?
choices: Measure ambient static pressure for the altimeter and VSI
Sense and deliver ram air pressure to the airspeed indicator and air data systems
Provide total air temperature data to the ADC for TAS computation
Generate angle-of-attack signals for stall warning systems
multipleChoice.explanation: The pitot tube senses ram (dynamic) pressure — the pressure created by the aircraft's forward motion through the air — and delivers this to the ASI and ADC.

practice.question: After installing a new pitot tube on a King Air, a technician needs to verify the installation is correct before re-certifying the pitot-static system. Which instrument should respond correctly when the technician applies a known positive pressure to the pitot fitting?
choices: Altimeter — it requires the pitot line for altitude correction
Vertical speed indicator — it uses both pitot and static for rate calculations
Airspeed indicator — it receives ram air from the pitot tube and should deflect in proportion to applied pressure
Attitude indicator — it uses a pitot-referenced vacuum for gyro spin
practice.explanation: Applying positive pressure to the pitot fitting simulates ram air. The ASI, which connects directly to the pitot tube, should deflect proportionally. The altimeter and VSI use only the static port.

preassessment.question: What does the pitot tube deliver to the airspeed indicator?
choices: Static pressure
Differential pressure
Total air temperature
Ram air pressure

questions.learningObjective: Altimeter measures barometric altitude via static pressure changes
questions.fullObjective: Explain that an altimeter measures barometric altitude by sensing static pressure changes and converting them to altitude in feet or meters referenced to a selected datum.

flashcard.definition: An altimeter measures barometric altitude by sensing changes in static (atmospheric) pressure through an aneroid capsule; as altitude increases, pressure decreases, and the capsule expands, rotating gears to display altitude in feet or meters referenced to the set datum.
flashcard.analogy: An altimeter is like a metal balloon inside a box — as you climb, outside pressure drops, the balloon expands, and a linked pointer shows how much it expanded, which corresponds to altitude.

jeopardy.question: The altimeter measures barometric altitude by sensing changes in this pressure through an aneroid capsule and converting expansion to a feet or meters reading.
choices: Ram air (pitot) pressure
Static atmospheric pressure
Differential cabin pressure
Total pressure from both pitot and static
jeopardy.explanation: The altimeter uses only static pressure — sensed through the static port — feeding an aneroid capsule that expands as static pressure decreases with altitude increase.

multipleChoice.question: How does a conventional altimeter determine and display barometric altitude?
choices: It measures ram pressure changes from the pitot tube and converts them to altitude
It calculates geometric altitude by triangulating GPS satellite signals
It senses static pressure through an aneroid capsule; pressure decreases with altitude, expanding the capsule to display altitude in feet
It receives digital altitude data from the ADC via a synchro motor system
multipleChoice.explanation: A conventional aneroid altimeter senses static (atmospheric) pressure changes. Decreasing pressure at higher altitude expands the aneroid capsule, and mechanical linkages convert the expansion to an altitude reading.

practice.question: During a pitot-static test, the technician increases simulated altitude on the tester. The altimeter reads correctly up to 10,000 ft then stops changing. The VSI shows zero rate. The ASI is functioning correctly on the pitot side. What does this indicate?
choices: A pitot tube blockage preventing the altimeter from receiving ram pressure above 10,000 ft
A magnetic compass deviation preventing altitude correlation
An ADC failure that stops altitude output above 10,000 ft
A static system leak or restriction between the tester and the altimeter, preventing pressure changes from reaching the capsule
practice.explanation: The altimeter and VSI both froze while the ASI (pitot-connected) continued working. A restriction in the static plumbing between tester and instrument would trap static pressure and prevent further altitude changes.

preassessment.question: What pressure does the altimeter sense to measure barometric altitude?
choices: Static atmospheric pressure
Ram air pressure
Differential pitot-static pressure
Cabin pressure

questions.learningObjective: Static port supplies altimeter, VSI, and ASI
questions.fullObjective: State that the static pressure system senses ambient atmospheric pressure through flush-mounted static ports and supplies this reference to the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator.

flashcard.definition: The aircraft static pressure system collects ambient atmospheric pressure through flush-mounted static ports and supplies it to three flight instruments: the altimeter, vertical speed indicator (VSI), and airspeed indicator (ASI).
flashcard.analogy: The static port is like a shared water main — one tap feeds three separate fixtures (altimeter, VSI, ASI) all from the same source of ambient pressure.

jeopardy.question: Flush-mounted static ports supply ambient atmospheric pressure to these three flight instruments: the altimeter, VSI, and one more.
choices: Magnetic compass
Attitude indicator
Airspeed indicator
Turn coordinator
jeopardy.explanation: The static pressure system feeds three instruments: the altimeter (altitude reference), the VSI (rate of pressure change), and the airspeed indicator (static side of differential pressure).

multipleChoice.question: Which flight instruments receive static pressure from the aircraft's static port system?
choices: Altimeter, VSI, and attitude indicator
Altimeter, attitude indicator, and turn coordinator
VSI, airspeed indicator, and heading indicator
Altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator
multipleChoice.explanation: The static system supplies three instruments: the altimeter (senses absolute static pressure for altitude), the VSI (senses rate of static pressure change), and the ASI (uses static as the reference side of the airspeed differential).

practice.question: After a heavy rainstorm, moisture entered the static system. The technician is troubleshooting erratic and sluggish readings on multiple instruments. Which set of instruments would be affected by static system contamination?
choices: Altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator — the static system instruments
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator — the gyro-driven instruments
Magnetic compass and attitude indicator — the direction-finding instruments
GPS and ADF — the navigation receivers
practice.explanation: Moisture in the static system affects the altimeter, VSI, and ASI — all three rely on clean static pressure to function accurately. Gyro instruments are unaffected.

preassessment.question: Which three instruments are connected to the aircraft static pressure system?
choices: AI, DG, TC
ALT, VSI, ASI
ALT, ASI, compass
VSI, TC, ALT

questions.learningObjective: Standard T arrangement of flight instruments
questions.fullObjective: State the standard 'T' arrangement of flight instruments: airspeed indicator upper-left, attitude indicator upper-center, altimeter upper-right, and heading indicator lower-center.

flashcard.definition: The FAA-standard 'Basic T' layout places four primary flight instruments in a T pattern: ASI (upper-left), AI (upper-center), ALT (upper-right), and heading indicator (lower-center).
flashcard.analogy: The 'T' arrangement is designed so a pilot's eye naturally scans from the center (AI) outward to the wings (ASI left, ALT right) then down to the stem (HI bottom) — like reading a plus sign from the center out.

jeopardy.question: In the standard 'Basic T' instrument layout, the attitude indicator occupies this position, with the airspeed indicator to its left and the altimeter to its right.
choices: Lower-center
Upper-right
Upper-left
Upper-center
jeopardy.explanation: The Basic T puts the AI at upper-center — the reference instrument for all cross-checks — with ASI upper-left, ALT upper-right, and DI/HI lower-center.

multipleChoice.question: What is the standard 'Basic T' arrangement of primary flight instruments?
choices: ASI upper-left, AI upper-center, ALT upper-right, HI lower-center
AI upper-left, ASI upper-center, ALT upper-right, HI lower-center
ASI upper-left, AI upper-center, HI upper-right, ALT lower-center
AI upper-left, HI upper-center, ASI upper-right, ALT lower-center
multipleChoice.explanation: The FAA Basic T: ASI (airspeed indicator) at upper-left, AI (attitude indicator) at upper-center, ALT (altimeter) at upper-right, and DI/HI (heading indicator) at lower-center.

practice.question: A technician is installing replacement instruments after an avionics upgrade. The aircraft must return to the FAA-standard Basic T arrangement. Where should the attitude indicator be mounted?
choices: Upper-left, adjacent to the airspeed indicator
Upper-center, as the primary reference instrument in the Basic T layout
Lower-center, with the heading indicator above it
Upper-right, with the altimeter moved to the left
practice.explanation: The Basic T places the attitude indicator at upper-center — it is the primary reference instrument around which all other T instruments are arranged.

preassessment.question: In the Basic T layout, where is the attitude indicator positioned?
choices: Upper-left
Upper-right
Upper-center
Lower-center

questions.learningObjective: Gyroscopic rigidity in space allows stable attitude reference
questions.fullObjective: Explain that gyroscopic rigidity in space is the principle that allows gyroscopic instruments to maintain a stable attitude reference by resisting any force that tries to change the orientation of the spinning rotor.

flashcard.definition: The property of a spinning gyro rotor to resist any force that tries to change its plane of rotation; once spinning, the rotor axis remains pointed in a fixed direction in space, providing a stable attitude reference.
flashcard.analogy: A spinning top stays upright and resists being knocked over — the faster it spins, the more it resists tilting. Gyroscopic instruments rely on this same principle to hold a fixed reference.

jeopardy.question: This gyroscopic property allows the attitude indicator's rotor to maintain a fixed orientation in space, resisting aircraft pitch and roll movements to provide a stable reference.
choices: Rigidity in space
Precession
Gyroscopic drift
Gyroscopic wander
jeopardy.explanation: Rigidity in space (also called gyroscopic inertia) causes a spinning rotor to resist any force changing its axis orientation. This resistance provides the stable reference needed for the attitude indicator.

multipleChoice.question: What gyroscopic principle allows the attitude indicator to maintain a stable pitch and roll reference as the aircraft maneuvers?
choices: Precession — the rotor tilts 90° from applied force to provide resistance
Rigidity in space — the spinning rotor resists any force trying to change its orientation
Gyroscopic drift — the systematic error accumulated over time provides a reference
Transport wander — the latitude-dependent effect provides a stabilizing force
multipleChoice.explanation: Gyroscopic rigidity in space means the spinning rotor maintains its axis orientation regardless of aircraft movement. The aircraft moves around the gyro, and the gyro's resistance to change is what provides the attitude reference.

practice.question: After a long international flight, a pilot reports that the vacuum-driven attitude indicator has drifted noticeably from actual attitude. Which gyroscopic characteristic best explains this phenomenon?
choices: Rigidity in space — the gyro maintained perfect orientation but the magnetic compass disagreed
Transport wander — excessive aircraft speed caused the attitude indicator to precess
Gyroscopic precession and real wander — imperfections in bearings and Earth's rotation cause the gyro to drift slowly from its initial orientation over time
Gyroscopic rigidity breaking down — the rotor slowed enough to lose its reference during descent
practice.explanation: While rigidity in space provides the stable reference, bearing friction and Earth's rotation cause real and apparent wander over time. On long flights, this drift accumulates, requiring periodic cage and re-erect.

preassessment.question: Which gyroscopic property allows the attitude indicator to resist changes in rotor orientation?
choices: Precession
Gyroscopic drift
Transport wander
Rigidity in space

questions.learningObjective: VSI showing descent in level flight indicates static system leak
questions.fullObjective: Given a VSI showing a constant rate of descent in level flight, determine that a leak in the static system allowing pressure to equalize faster than the instrument's calibrated orifice is the most likely cause.

flashcard.definition: A VSI showing a constant descent rate in level flight indicates a leak in the static system that allows static pressure to equalize faster than the calibrated orifice; the VSI interprets this as a pressure increase (descent) even when the aircraft is level.
flashcard.analogy: The VSI is like a sealed jar with a tiny leak hole measuring pressure change rate. If you punch a bigger hole (leak), pressure equalizes too fast — the instrument thinks you're descending into higher pressure, even if you're not moving.

jeopardy.question: A VSI consistently shows a rate of descent in level flight because this fault allows static pressure to equalize faster than the instrument's calibrated metering orifice.
choices: A blocked pitot tube
A leak in the static system
A failed gyro erection system
An over-sensitive aneroid capsule
jeopardy.explanation: A static system leak provides a path for pressure to equalize faster than the VSI's calibrated orifice. The VSI senses the higher pressure influx as an apparent descent, even in level flight.

multipleChoice.question: A VSI indicates a constant 200 fpm descent while the aircraft is confirmed to be in level flight. The altimeter reads correctly. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The altimeter is correct but the VSI aneroid capsule spring has weakened
A blockage in the pitot tube is causing backpressure that affects the VSI
A leak in the static system is allowing external pressure to enter faster than the calibrated orifice, making the VSI sense a false pressure rise (descent)
The VSI's calibrated orifice has become enlarged, making the instrument over-sensitive
multipleChoice.explanation: A static leak allows outside pressure to bleed into the static system faster than normal. The VSI compares the two sides of its differential — the faster pressure equalisation looks like a descent signal.

practice.question: After an aircraft wash, a technician notices the VSI indicates a slight but constant rate of descent during ground runs even though the aircraft is stationary. The altimeter appears stable. What is the most likely finding on inspection?
choices: Water in the pitot line is causing back-pressure that registers on the VSI
An altimeter setting error is causing the VSI to show an apparent descent
The VSI pivot or bearings have seized, causing the pointer to rest below zero
A static port gasket or connection in the static plumbing has developed a leak, allowing ambient pressure to equalize faster than the VSI orifice
practice.explanation: After a wash, water intrusion or disturbed seals are common. A static system leak (disturbed port gasket, loose fitting) allows external pressure to enter the system faster than the VSI orifice, showing a false descent.

preassessment.question: What fault causes a VSI to show constant descent in level flight?
choices: Static system leak
Blocked pitot tube
Vacuum pump failure
Blocked static port

questions.learningObjective: Altimeter, VSI, and ASI rely on static port
questions.fullObjective: State that the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator are the flight instruments that rely on the static port for accurate pressure readings.

flashcard.definition: Three flight instruments depend on the static port for accurate readings: the altimeter (senses static pressure for altitude), the VSI (senses rate of static pressure change), and the airspeed indicator (uses static as the reference side of the pitot-static differential).
flashcard.analogy: The static port is like a shared electrical ground — it provides a common reference that three different instruments need to make accurate measurements.

jeopardy.question: These three flight instruments all rely on an accurate static port reading: the altimeter, the VSI, and this third instrument.
choices: Attitude indicator
Heading indicator
Airspeed indicator
Turn coordinator
jeopardy.explanation: The ASI uses static pressure as the reference side of its differential measurement. Along with the altimeter and VSI, all three require a clean, accurate static port connection.

multipleChoice.question: Which three instruments rely on the static port for accurate pressure readings?
choices: Airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, and VSI
Altimeter, attitude indicator, and airspeed indicator
VSI, turn coordinator, and altimeter
Altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator
multipleChoice.explanation: The static port supplies ambient pressure to the altimeter (altitude), VSI (rate of altitude change), and ASI (reference side of the airspeed differential). All three fail or give erroneous readings with a blocked or leaking static port.

practice.question: A ferry pilot reports three instruments behaving erratically at altitude after flying through heavy icing. Which combination of instruments is consistent with an iced-over static port?
choices: Altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator — the static system instruments
